### Unit 3 Refrigeration and Refrigerants

refrigerant further cools the liquid refrigerant that remains
as the refrigerant flows through the metering device. This
quick drop in pressure in the metering device lowers the boiling point or saturation temperature of the liquid leaving the
metering device. Although flash gas helps to cool the liquid
refrigerant in the evaporator, too much flash gas can result in
a loss in system capacity because less liquid is now available to
boil to vapor in the evaporator when cooling the refrigerated
space. Because of this, flash gas should be kept to a minimum.
Several types of metering devices for many applications
are available. They include the capillary tube, the automatic
expansion valve, the thermostatic expansion valve, and the
electronic expansion valve.

#### 3.11 A High-Temperature,
#### Matched Refrigeration
#### System By The Numbers
We have described the basic components of the mechanical,
vapor-compression refrigeration system according to function. These components must be properly matched for each
specific application. For instance, a low-temperature compressor cannot be used in a high-temperature application
because of the pumping characteristics of the compressor.

Some equipment can be mixed and matched successfully by
using the manufacturer’s data, but only someone with considerable knowledge and experience should do so. What
follows is a description of a matched, high-temperature, system correctly working at design conditions. Later we will
explain malfunctions and adverse operating conditions.
A typical home air-conditioning system operating at a
design temperature of 75°F inside temperature has a relative
humidity (moisture content of the conditioned room air) of
50%. The air in the house gives up heat to the refrigerant.
The indoor coil is also responsible for removing some of the
moisture from the air to keep the humidity at an acceptable
level. This is known as dehumidifying.
Moisture removal requires considerable energy. Approximately the same amount (970!Btu) of latent heat removal is
required to condense a pound of water vapor from the air as to
condense a pound of steam. All air-conditioning systems must
have a method for dealing with this moisture after it has turned
to a liquid. Some units drip, some drain the liquid into plumbing waste drains, some use a slinger ring on the condenser fan,
and some use the liquid at the outdoor coil to help the system
capacity by evaporating it at the condenser. Remember that
part of the system is inside the house and part of the system is
outside the house. The numbers in the following description
correspond to the circled numbers in Figure!3.31.

Figure 3.31 A typical R-410A air-conditioning system showing temperatures and airflow. Red indicates warm/hot refrigerant; blue indicates cool/cold
refrigerant.

## PDF Page 56

56

Section 1 Theory of Heat

1. A mixture of 75% liquid and 25% vapor leaves the
metering device and enters the evaporator.
2. The mixture is saturated R-410A at a pressure of
118!psig, which corresponds to a 40°F boiling point. It
is important to remember that the pressure is 118! psig
because the evaporating refrigerant is boiling at 40°F.
3. The mixture tumbles through the evaporator with the
liquid evaporating from the 75°F heat and humidity load
of the inside air as it moves along.
4. When the mixture is about halfway through the coil, the
refrigerant is composed of about 50% liquid and 50%
vapor and is still at the same temperature and pressure
because a change of state is taking place. Remember that
this is a latent heat transfer.
5. The refrigerant is now 100% vapor. In other words, it
has reached the 100% saturation point of the vapor.
Recall the example using the saturated water table; at
various points the water was saturated with heat. That
is, if any heat is removed at that point, some of the vapor
changes back to a liquid; if any heat is added, the temperature of the vapor rises. This rise in temperature of
the vapor makes it a superheated vapor. (Superheat is
sensible heat.) At point 5, the saturated vapor is still at
40°F and still able to absorb more heat from the 75°F
room air.
6. The pure vapor that now exists is normally superheated
about 10°F above the saturation temperature. Examine
the line in Figure!3.31 at this point to see that the temperature is about 50°F.
NOTE: To arrive at the correct
superheat reading, take the following steps:
A. Note the suction pressure or evaporating pressure reading
from the suction, or low-side, gauge: 118!psig.
B. Convert the suction pressure reading to suction or
evaporating temperature using the temperature/pressure
chart for R-410A: 40°F.
C. Use a suitable thermometer to record the actual
temperature of the suction line at the outlet of the
evaporator: 50°F.
D. Subtract the saturated suction temperature from the
actual suction line temperature: 50°F 2 40°F 5 10°F of
superheat. -
The vapor is said to be heat laden because it contains the
heat removed from the room air. The heat was absorbed
into the vaporizing refrigerant that boiled off to vapor as
it traveled through the evaporator. The vapor superheats
another 10°F and is now 60°F as it travels down the suction line to the compressor. The superheat that is picked
up only in the evaporator is referred to as evaporator
superheat; the total superheat that is picked up in the
evaporator and the suction line is referred to as system
superheat. To calculate the system superheat, we measure
the suction line temperature (from item C, above) close
to the compressor’s inlet instead of at the outlet of the
evaporator. In this example, the evaporator superheat is

10°F (50°F 2 40°F) and the system superheat is 20°F
(60°F 2 40°F). System superheat is often referred to as
compressor superheat.
7. The vapor is drawn into the compressor by its pumping action, which creates a low-pressure suction. When
the vapor left the evaporator, its temperature was about
50°F with 10°F of superheat above the saturated boiling
temperature of 40°F. As the vapor moves along toward
the compressor, it is contained in the suction line, which
is usually copper and should be insulated to keep it from
drawing heat into the system from the surroundings and
to prevent it from sweating. However, it still picks up
some heat. Because the suction line carries vapor, any
heat that it picks up will quickly raise the temperature.
Remember that it does not take much sensible heat to
raise the temperature of a vapor. Depending on the length
of the line and the quality of the insulation, the suction
line temperature may be 60°F at the compressor inlet.
8. Highly superheated gas leaves the compressor through
the hot gas line on the high-pressure side of the system. This line normally is very short because the condenser is usually close to the compressor. On a hot day
the hot gas line may be close to 200°F with a pressure
of 446! psig. Because the saturated temperature corresponding to 446! psig is 125°F, the hot gas line has
about 75°F (200°F 2 125°F) of superheat that must be
removed before condensing can occur. Because the line
is so hot and a vapor is present, the line will give up
heat readily to the surroundings. The surrounding air
temperature is 95°F.
9. The superheat has been removed and the refrigerant has
cooled down to the 125°F condensing temperature. Point
9 is the point in the system where the desuperheating
vapor has just cooled to a temperature of 125°F; this
is referred to as a 100% saturated vapor point. If any
heat is taken away or rejected, liquid will start to form.
As more heat is rejected, the remaining saturated vapor
will continue to condense to saturated liquid at the condensing temperature of 125°F. Now notice that the coil
temperature is corresponding to the high-side pressure
of 446! psig and 125°F. The high-pressure reading of
278! psig is due to the refrigerant condensing at 125°F.
In fact, 446!psig is the vapor pressure that the vapor is
exerting on the liquid while it is condensing. Remember,
it is vapor pressure that the gauge reads.
10. The conditions for condensing are determined by the
efficiency of the condenser. In this example we use a
standard condenser, which has a condensing temperature about 30°F higher than the surrounding air used to
absorb heat from the condenser. In this example, 95°F
outside air is absorbing the heat, so 95°F 1 30°F 5
125°F condensing temperature. Some condensers will
condense at 25°F above the surrounding air temperature; these are high-efficiency condensers and the highpressure side of the system will be operating at a lower


---

## PDF Page 57

### Unit 3 Refrigeration and Refrigerants

pressure. Condensing temperatures and pressures are
also dependent on the heat load given to the condenser
to reject. The higher the heat load, the higher will be
the condensing temperature and the corresponding
pressure. It can also be concluded that as the outside
temperature rises, the operating pressure on the high
side of the system will also rise. For example, if the outside ambient (surrounding) temperature rises to 105°F,
the condenser saturation temperature on our sample
system would rise to about 135°F.
11. The refrigerant is now 100% liquid at the saturated
temperature of 125°F. As the liquid continues along
the coil, the air continues to cool the liquid to below
the actual condensing temperature. The liquid may go
as much as 20°F below the condensing temperature of
125°F before it reaches the metering device. Any liquid
at a temperature below the condensing temperature of
125°F is called subcooled liquid. In this example, the
liquid is cooled to 105°F before it reaches the metering
device. The liquid now has 20°F (125°F 2 105°F) of
subcooling.
12. The liquid refrigerant reaches the metering device
through a pipe, usually copper, from the condenser. This
liquid line is often field installed and not insulated. Since
the temperature of the liquid line is warmer than the temperature of the surrounding air, keeping the liquid line
uninsulated allows some additional heat to be rejected
by the refrigerant into the surrounding air, which helps
increase the operating efficiency of the system. Since the
liquid line may be long, and depending on the distance
between the condenser and the metering device, the
amount of additional heat being rejected may be significant. Heat given up here is leaving the system, and that
is good. The refrigerant entering the metering device may
be as much as 20°F cooler than the condensing temperature of 125°F, so the liquid line entering the metering
device may be 105°F.
13. The refrigerant entering the metering device is 100%
subcooled liquid. In the short length of the metering
device’s orifice (a pinhole about the size of a small sewing needle), the subcooled liquid is changed to a mixture of about 75% saturated liquid and 25% saturated
vapor. The ratio of liquid to vapor leaving the metering
device depends on both the system and the application.
The 25% vapor, known as flash gas, is used to cool the
remaining 75% of the liquid down to 40°F, the boiling
temperature of the evaporator. The cooling performed
by flash gas is a system loss because it cools the liquid
temperature down to the 40°F evaporating temperature.
The cooling is therefore wasted because it is not performed in the evaporator to lower the temperature of the
inside air and remove humidity. The only way to minimize flash gas is to get the temperature of the subcooled
liquid entering the metering device closer to the evaporating temperature.

57

The refrigerant has now completed one full cycle. It should
be evident that a refrigerant does the same thing over and over,
changing from a liquid to a vapor in the evaporator and back to
a liquid in the condenser. The expansion device meters the flow
to the evaporator, and the compressor pumps the refrigerant out
of the evaporator.
The following list briefly summarizes the refrigeration
cycle:
1. The evaporator absorbs heat into the system.
2. The compressor pumps the heat-laden vapor.
3. The condenser rejects heat from the system.
4. The expansion device meters the flow of refrigerant.

#### 3.12 Refrigerants
Previously, we have used water and R-410A as examples
of refrigerants. Although many products have the characteristics of a refrigerant, we will cover only a few here.
Unit!9, “Refrigerant and Oil Chemistry and Management—
Recovery, Recycling, Reclaiming, and Retrofitting,” provides
more detailed information.
The following four refrigerants either can no longer
be!manufactured:
R-12—Used primarily in medium- and high-temperature
refrigeration applications. Manufacturing and importing of this refrigerant was banned as of January 1,
1996.
R-22—Used primarily in residential, commercial, and
industrial air-conditioning applications and in some
commercial and industrial refrigeration. R-22 was
phased out in new equipment in 2010, and total production ended in 2020.
R-500—Used primarily in older air-conditioning applications and some commercial refrigeration. Manufacturing and importing of this refrigerant was
banned as of January 1, 1996.
R-502—Used primarily in low-temperature refrigeration
applications. Manufacturing and importing of this
refrigerant was banned as of January 1, 1996. It is an
azeotropic refrigerant blend that has negligible temperature glide and behaves like a pure compound.
The following are some of the more popular refrigerants,
even though some of them may be replaced in the near future
with substances that have lower global warming potentials
(GWPs). Note: At the time of this writing, the Environmental
Protection Agency (EPA) has proposed rules for refrigerant
substitutes. The rules involve a change in the listing status
for certain refrigerant substitutes under EPA Section 612,
the Significant New Alternatives Policy Program (SNAP).
Some of the refrigerants and refrigerant blends listed below
may be affected when this proposed rule takes effect. Many
refrigerants and refrigerant blends that have high GWPs will
no longer be allowed to be used even as retrofit refrigerants.


---

## PDF Page 58

58

Section 1 Theory of Heat

These refrigerants and blends will still be listed as follows
because they exist in HVACR equipment from past installations and retrofits. It is important that service technicians
understand what these refrigerants and blends consist of
for service purposes. It is the service technician’s responsibility to keep abreast with the most recent laws and rules
concerning refrigerants use and service. Be sure to visit the
EPA website, www.epa.gov, for the most up-to-date information regarding refrigerant-related regulations.
R-134a—Properties very similar to R-12. Used primarily in medium- and high-temperature refrigeration
applications, refrigerators and freezers, and automotive air-conditioning. It is a replacement for R-12
but not a direct one because retrofitting is required.
The ester-based lubricants used with R-134a are not
compatible with the oils typically used in R-12 systems. R-134a does not deplete stratospheric ozone,
but does contribute to global warming.
R-402A—A near-azeotropic interim HCFC-based refrigerant retrofit blend of R-125, R-290, and R-22 for
low- and medium-temperature commercial refrigeration applications. Replaces R-502.
R-404A—R-404A has slightly higher working pressures
than R-502. A near-azeotropic refrigerant blend with
a small temperature glide. Eliminated from new supermarket refrigeration systems, remote condensing
units, and some stand-alone equipment because of its
high GWP. Some new lower-GWP refrigerants, such
as R-448A, R-449A, R-450A, and R-513A, all have
less than half the GWP of the candidates they are
replacing.
R-407A—A retrofit refrigerant blend for R-22 in mediumand low-temperature commercial refrigeration applications. It is not recommended for air-conditioning
applications.
R-407C—Similar properties to R-22. It is replacing R-22
in residential and commercial air-conditioning applications. It can be used as a retrofit refrigerant for
R-22. A near-azeotropic refrigerant blend, R-407C,
like R-134a, operates with ester-based lubricants
that are not compatible with the oils typically used
in R-22 systems. R-407C is a great choice when the
condensing unit of a system must be replaced, as most
of the refrigeration oil is contained in the compressor.
R-407C can also be used in-medium temperature
refrigeration systems.
R-408A—A near-azeotropic interim HCFC-based refrigerant retrofit blend of R-125, R-143a, and R-22 for
low- and medium-temperature commercial refrigeration applications. Replaces R-502.
R-410A—A near-azeotropic refrigerant blend replacing
R-22 in residential and commercial air-conditioning
applications. It has much higher operating pressures

than R-22 and special safety concerns. R-410A has
a very small temperature glide and is not recommended as a retrofit refrigerant. Although R-410A
does not deplete stratospheric ozone, it is classified
as a super-polluter given its high GWP.
#### SAFETY
PRECAUTION: Never add R-410A to a system that was
manufactured for use with R-22. R-22 system components
are typically not manufactured to accept the higher operating pressures that are present in R-410A systems. For example, the shells of R-410A compressors are manufactured with
thicker steel than are the shells of R-22 compressors.-
R-417A—A near-azeotropic retrofit refrigerant blend
for R-22 direct expansion air-conditioning systems.
It is best suited to replace R-22 in smaller split airconditioning systems. It is an efficient and cost effective, non-ozone depleting HFC-based refrigerant
blend. It is compatible with mineral oil, alkylbenzene,
and polyol ester lubricants. It has lower discharge
temperatures and pressures than R-22.
R-438A—A near-azeotropic refrigerant blend for use as
a retrofit refrigerant for R-22 with minimum system
change. R-438A is a blend of five different refrigerants.
It can be used for retrofitting R-22 direct expansion
air-conditioning (high-temperature) applications and
medium- and low-temperature refrigeration applications. It has a small percentage of hydrocarbons in its
blend to help in oil return. This makes it compatible
with mineral oils. R-438A is nonflammable and has an
ASHRAE safety group classification of A1. Its ozone
depletion potential (ODP) is zero. The evaporator and
condenser temperature glide is typically in the 6°F to7°F
temperature range. It is not recommended for flooded
evaporators or systems with centrifugal compressors.
R-422A—A near-azeotropic retrofit refrigerant blend for
R-502 and R-502 service blends in commercial refrigeration applications. Consists of R-125, R-134a, and
R-600a.
R-422B—A near-azeotropic retrofit refrigerant blend for
R-22 in air-conditioning.
R-422C—A near-azeotropic retrofit refrigerant blend for
R-502 and R-502 service blends in commercial refrigeration applications.
R-422D—A near-azeotropic refrigerant retrofit blend for
R-22 in commercial refrigeration applications.
R-427A—A near-azeotropic refrigerant retrofit blend
for R-22 in air-conditioning and commercial refrigeration, but preferred for commercial refrigeration
applications.
R-507—A replacement for R-502 in low- and mediumtemperature refrigeration applications. R-507 is an
azeotropic mixture of R-125 and R-143a. Has slightly
higher pressures and capacity than R-404A.
R-507A—An azeotropic mixture of R-125 and R-143a.
It has a higher efficiency than R-404A and also a


---

## PDF Page 59

### Unit 3 Refrigeration and Refrigerants

higher efficiency than low-temperature R-22. Polyol
ester lubricant must be used with R-507A. Eliminated
from new supermarket refrigeration systems, remote
condensing units, and some stand-alone equipment
because of its high GWP.
HFO-1234yf—A low GWP replacement for R-134a
intended for use in mobile air-conditioning (MAC) systems in the automotive industry. It is currently being
used in this application today in some MAC systems.
HFO-1234yf does not deplete stratospheric ozone.
HFO-1234-ze—Intended to replace R-410A in residential and light commercial air-conditioning and heat
pump applications while offering a 75% reduction
in GWP. It also offers excellent energy efficiency, is
cost effective, and can be used in existing equipment
design with minimal changes. HFO-1234ze does not
deplete stratospheric ozone. HFO-1234ze also has
significant advantages over R-32, which has medium
GWPs and high discharge temperatures. High discharge temperatures can affect system performance
and durability in hot climates. Also, oil changes may
be needed with R-32.
Hydrofluoro-olefin (HFO) refrigerants are widely
recognized as the next generation of refrigerants
because of their environmental friendliness, costeffectiveness, and great energy efficiencies. Refer to
Unit 9, “Refrigerant and Oil Chemistry and Management,” for more information on hydrofluoroolefin (HFO) and hydrocarbon (HC) refrigerants.
As we will see later in this unit, the choice of refrigerant is becoming more important because of environmental issues. Some refrigerants, when released to the
atmosphere, are causing damage to the ozone layer in
the stratosphere, 7 to 30! miles above earth’s surface.
Refrigerants are also being blamed for global warming
effects.

59

in a room with an open gas flame, such as a pilot light on a
gas water heater or furnace, the equipment must be kept leak
free. If the refrigerant escapes and gets to the flame, toxic and
corrosive gases can be created. These gases can burn the eyes
and nose, and severely hamper the breathing of anyone in the
room.-

#### 3.14 Refrigerants Must
#### BE!DETECTABLE
Refrigerants must be readily detectable. For detecting some
large leaks, the first thing to do is to listen for the hiss of escaping refrigerant. In some cases, some leaks may be so small they
cannot be heard by the human ear. However, many leaks can
be found by listening. There is an ultrasonic leak-detecting
device on the market that detects leaks by sound, Figure!3.32.
This leak detector enables the technician to hear the sound of
the fluid moving through the piping circuit toward the location of the leak. The pitch of the emitted sound changes as the
sensor is brought closer and closer to the leak.
Soap bubbles are a practical and yet simple leak detector.
Many service technicians use commercially prepared products
that will blow large elastic-type bubbles at the point of the
leak, Figure 3.33. These products are valuable when the technician knows a leak is in a certain area. Soap-bubble solution can
be applied with a brush to the tubing joint to see exactly where
the leak is. When refrigerant lines are below freezing temperature, a small amount of antifreeze can be added to the bubble
solution. At times, a piece of equipment can be submerged in
water to watch for bubbles, which is effective when applicable.
A halide leak detector, Figure 3.34,!uses an acetylene or
propane fueled flame to heat a copper disk, which is then
Figure 3.32 Ultrasonic leak detector. Photo by Bill Johnson

#### 3.13 Refrigerants Must
#### BE!SAFE
To protect people from sickness or injury, even death,
refrigerants must be safe in the event they escape from a system. For instance, using ammonia for the air-conditioning
system in a public place could be a disaster even though
ammonia is an efficient refrigerant. Modern refrigerants
are nontoxic, and equipment is designed to use a minimum
amount of refrigerant to accomplish its job. A window air
conditioner, for example, normally contains less than 2!lb of
refrigerant, while household refrigerators typically contain
only a few ounces of refrigerant.
#### Safety Precaution:
Because refrigerants are heavier than air, proper ventilation is important. Refrigerants should not be exposed to open
flames. When refrigeration equipment or cylinders are located


---

## PDF Page 60

60

Section 1 Theory of Heat

Figure 3.33 Soap-bubble solution.

Figure 3.35 Electronic leak detector. Courtesy Ferris State University.
Photo by John Tomczyk

Figure 3.36 Ultraviolet leak detection equipment.! Courtesy of Spectronics Corporation, Westbury, NY

Figure 3.34 Halide torch. Courtesy of Uniweld Products

used to! detect chlorinated refrigerant gas leaks. It operates
on the principle that when the refrigerant is exposed to an
open flame in the presence of glowing copper, the flame will
change color. The halide leak detector should be used in wellventilated areas, because the resulting blue or green flame
indicates that toxic gas is being produced. Although the
amounts produced can be very small, the gas is an irritant—
corrosive as well as toxic.
The electronic leak detector in Figure!3.35 is battery operated, is small enough to be easily carried, and has a flexible
probe. The refrigerant charge specifications for some residential air-conditioning equipment call for half-ounce accuracy.
The electronic leak detectors are capable of detecting leak
rates down to a quarter of an ounce per year.
Another detection system uses a high-intensity ultraviolet
lamp, Figure!3.36. An additive introduced into the refrigerant

system will show as a bright yellow-green glow under the
ultraviolet lamp at the source of the leak. After repairing the
leak and wiping the area clean with a general-purpose cleaner,
the area can be reinspected—the additive remains in the system. Should a new leak be suspected at a later date, it will still
show as a yellow-green color under the ultraviolet light. This
system will detect leaks as small as a quarter of an ounce per
year.

#### 3.15 The Boiling Point
#### OF!THE!REFRIGERANT
The boiling point of a refrigerant should be low at atmospheric pressure so that low temperatures may be obtained
without going into a vacuum. For example, R-410A can be


---

## PDF Page 61

61

### Unit 3 Refrigeration and Refrigerants

boiled as low as 261°F before the boiling pressure goes
into a vacuum, whereas R-12 can be boiled only as low
as 221°F before it goes into a vacuum. Water would have
to be boiled at 29.67! in. Hg vacuum just to boil at 40°F.
NOTE: When using the compound gauge below atmospheric pressure, the number scale is different from the one that
is used above atmospheric pressure. Above atmospheric pressure, the units of pressure are psig, while below atmospheric
pressure, the units of pressure (vacuum) are inches of mercury.
In the vacuum region, the gauge starts at atmospheric pressure
(0 psig) and continues down to a perfect vacuum (29.92 in. Hg).
When possible, design engineers avoid using refrigerants that
must be in a vacuum to boil at the desired temperature. When a
system operates in a vacuum and a leak occurs, the atmosphere
is pulled inside the system instead of the refrigerant leaking out
of the system.-

#### 3.16 Pumping Characteristics
Pumping characteristics have to do with how much refrigerant vapor is pumped per amount of work accomplished.
Water was disqualified as a practical refrigerant for small
equipment partly for this reason. One pound of water at 40°F
has a vapor, or specific, volume of 2445!ft3 compared to about
0.5! ft3 for R-410A. This means that the system capacity is
directly related to the number of pounds of refrigerant that
are circulated through the system per unit of time. This is
expressed in units called pounds per minute, or lb/min. Since
the vapor volume of 40°F water is so high, the compressor would have to move 2445!ft3 of vapor to move a single
pound. Thus, the compressor would have to be very large for
a water system. Modern refrigerants meet all of these requirements better than any of the older types.

#### 3.17 Popular Refrigerants
#### And Their Important
#### CHARACTERISTICS
The American National Standards Institute (ANSI) and the
American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers (ASHRAE) are responsible for naming
refrigerants and identifying their characteristics. Figure!3.37
is an organized list of some of the more popular refrigerants
and their characteristics. For more detailed information on
refrigerants, refer to Unit!9, “Refrigerant and Oil Chemistry
and Management—Recovery, Recycling, Reclaiming, and
Retrofitting.”
As mentioned earlier, environmental issues like
ozone depletion and global warming have forced manufacturing
phaseout dates for many refrigerants. However, these refrigerants
can still be used if recovered or recycled, or if they are in an operating refrigeration or air-conditioning system. Environmental issues
and phaseout dates have made many refrigerants very expensive

due to the heavy taxes imposed, and so alternative (environmentally friendly) refrigerants have entered the market. It is illegal to
intentionally vent any refrigerant into the atmosphere. Stiff fines
of up to $44,539 and/or imprisonment can follow. Because of
this, mandatory technician certification programs have educated
HVACR personnel on environmental issues, alternative refrigerants, and legislation issues. Visit the EPA’s website for the most upto-date information regarding refrigerant-related regulations.

#### 3.19 Recovery, Recycling,
#### And Reclaiming Of
#### REFRIGERANTS
It is mandatory for technicians to recover and sometimes recycle
refrigerants during installation and servicing operations to help
reduce emissions of chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs), hydrochlorofluorocarbons (HCFCs), and hydrofluorocarbons (HFCs) to the atmosphere. Examples of recovery equipment are shown in Figure!3.39.
Many larger systems can be fitted with receivers or dump tanks
into which the refrigerant can be pumped and stored while the
system is serviced. However, in smaller-capacity systems it is not
often feasible to provide these components. Recovery units or other
storage devices may be necessary. Most recovery and/or recycling units vary in technology and capabilities, so manufacturers’
instructions must be followed carefully when using this equipment.
Unit!9, “Refrigerant and Oil Chemistry and Management—Recovery,
Recycling, Reclaiming, and Retrofitting,” includes a detailed description of the recovery, recycling, and reclaiming of refrigerants.

## PDF Page 64

64

Section 1 Theory of Heat

but this is not absolutely accurate because it refers to the
heat content above the selected reference point. Refer back
to Figure! 1.15, the heat/temperature graph for water. We
used 0°F as the starting point of heat for water, knowing that
you can really remove more heat from the water (ice) and
lower the temperature below 0°F. We described the process as
the amount of heat added starting at 0°F. This heat is called
enthalpy. The pressure/enthalpy diagram is a similar diagram,
available for all refrigerants and sometimes referred to as a
p-e (pressure/enthalpy) or p-h (pressure/heat) chart. Since
different refrigerants have different characteristics, properties, and temperature/pressure relationships, the pressure/
enthalpy chart for each refrigerant is different. The pressure/
enthalpy chart is used to plot the complete refrigeration cycle
as a continuous loop.
The selected reference point for measuring the heat content in a refrigerant is 240°F. The pressure/enthalpy chart in
Figure!3.41 shows that the enthalpy, or heat content, in Btu/lb
(along the bottom of the chart) has a value of 0! Btu/lb
when the refrigerant is a saturated liquid at 240°F. The
heat content for temperature readings below 240°F saturated liquid are indicated as being negative. As we move
from left to right on the chart, the heat content per pound
of refrigerant increases. As we move from right to left, the
heat content per pound of refrigerant decreases.
As you inspect the pressure/enthalpy chart in Figure!3.40,
you will notice that there is a horseshoe-shaped curve in the

center of the chart. This curve is called the saturation curve
and contains the same information that is contained on the
temperature/pressure chart discussed earlier. The only difference is that the pressures on the pressure/enthalpy chart are
expressed in absolute pressures, psia, instead of gauge pressures, psig.
Any time a point lies on, or under, this curve, the refrigerant is saturated and will have a corresponding temperature
and pressure relationship. There are two saturation lines on
the chart. The saturated liquid line makes up the left boundary of the saturation curve. If while on this line, heat is added,
the refrigerant will start changing state to a vapor; if heat is
removed, the liquid will be subcooled. The saturated vapor
line makes up the right boundary of saturation curve. If,
while on this line, heat is added, the vapor will superheat;
if heat is removed, the vapor will start changing state to a
liquid. The saturated liquid and vapor lines meet at the top of
the saturation curve. This point is called the critical temperature or critical pressure. Above this point, the refrigerant will
not condense. It is a vapor regardless of how much pressure
is applied.
The area between the saturated liquid and saturated
vapor lines, inside the horseshoe-shaped curve, is where the
change of state occurs. Whenever a point lies between the
saturation lines, the refrigerant is in the partial liquid, partial
vapor state. The slanted, near-vertical lines between the saturated liquid and saturated vapor lines are the constant quality

### Unit 3 Refrigeration and Refrigerants

lines and describe the ratio of vapor to liquid in the mixture
between the saturation points. There are nine of these lines
under the saturation curve, each of which represents 10%.
The saturated liquid line on the left side of the curve represents 0% vapor and 100% liquid, while the saturated vapor
line on the right side of the saturation curve represents 100%
vapor and 0% liquid. The nine constant quality lines that are
located under the saturation curve are labeled, from left to
right, 0.1 through 0.9. These figures represent the percentage
of quality. Percentage of quality means percentage of vapor.
This means that if a point falls on the 0.2 constant quality
line, it would be 20% vapor and 80% liquid. If the plot is
closer to the saturated liquid curve, there is more liquid than
vapor. If the plot is closer to the saturated vapor curve, there
is more vapor than liquid. For example, let’s find a point on
the chart at 60°F (horizontal line under the saturation curve)
and 45!Btu/lb (along the bottom), Figure!3.42. This point is
underneath the horseshoe-shaped curve and lies on the 0.1
constant quality line. This means that the refrigerant is 90%
liquid and 10% vapor. Figure! 3.43 summarizes, in skeletal
form, the important regions, points, and lines of the pressure/
enthalpy diagram. For practical reasons, we will use only a

few of these skeletal forms for illustrating the functions of the
refrigeration cycle.
A refrigeration cycle is plotted in Figure!3.44. The system
to be plotted is an air-conditioning system using R-410A. On
this chart, five important points have been identified:
-

-

-

metering device
Point B: Outlet of the metering device and the inlet of
the evaporator
Point C: Outlet of the evaporator and the start of the
suction line
Point D: End of the suction line and inlet of the
compressor
Point E: Outlet of the compressor and start of the
condenser

The system is operating at 120°F condensing temperature (417.4!psig or 432.1!psia discharge pressure) and at an
evaporating temperature of 40°F (118.3! psig or 133! psia
suction pressure). The cycle plotted has 20°F of subcooling, as calculated by the difference between the condenser
saturation temperature of 120°F and the 100°F condenser

outlet, which is indicated by the point where the vertical line on the left side of the plot, crosses the saturation curve, Figure 3.45. There is 10°F of superheat in the
evaporator with another 10°F of superheat absorbed by
the refrigerant in the suction line as it returns to the compressor. The temperature of the suction gas entering the
compressor is 60°F, Figure 3.46. The inlet of the compressor was identified as point D in Figure!3.44. The compressor is air-cooled, and the suction gas enters the suction
valve adjacent to the cylinders. Assume that the hot gas
line is very short and its heat of rejection is negligible.
The following eight steps summarize the basic refrigeration
cycle (refer to Figure!3.44):

Figure 3.45 The condenser outlet is identified by the point the expansion device line crosses the saturated liquid line on the left side of the
pressure/enthalpy chart.

1. Refrigerant R-410A enters the expansion valve as a
subcooled liquid at 432.1! psia (417.4! psig) and 100°F,
point A. The heat content is 53!Btu/lb entering the expansion valve and 53! Btu/lb leaving the expansion valve.
Figure!3.44 shows the metering device represented by a
vertical line at the left side of the plot. It can therefore be
concluded that even though the temperature and pressure
of the refrigerant drops as it flows through the expansion valve, the heat content of the refrigerant remains
the same. It is important that heat content (Btu/lb) is not
confused with temperature. Note that the temperature of
the liquid refrigerant before the valve is 100°F, and the
temperature leaving the valve is 40°F. The temperature
drop can be accounted for by observing that we have
100% liquid entering the valve and about 75% liquid
leaving the valve. About 25% of the liquid has changed
to a vapor (flash gas). During the process of evaporating,

heat is absorbed from the remaining liquid, lowering
its temperature to 40°F. Remember, flash gas does not
contribute to the net refrigeration effect. The net refrigeration effect (NRE) is expressed in Btu/lb and is the
quantity of heat that each pound of refrigerant absorbs
from the refrigerated space to produce useful cooling.
2. Flash gas is a result of the pressure drop across the metering device. No enthalpy is gained or lost in this process,
which further explains why the expansion line from
point A to point B is vertical. This expansion process is
called adiabatic expansion because it happened at a constant enthalpy. Adiabatic processes, by definition, result
in temperature and pressure changes with no change in
heat content.
3. Usable refrigeration starts at point B where the refrigerant has a heat content of 53!Btu/lb. As heat is added
to the refrigerant in the evaporator, the refrigerant

40

gradually changes state to a vapor. Notice that as we
move toward the right from point B along a horizontal
line, the pressure remains unchanged but the heat content increases. The source of this increase in enthalpy is
the heat in the air that is being cooled by the evaporator.
All liquid has changed to a vapor when it reaches the
saturated vapor line. A small amount of sensible heat is
added to the refrigerant in the form of superheat (10°F)
between the saturated vapor line and point C. When the

## PDF Page 69

### Unit 3 Refrigeration and Refrigerants

not come from the conditioned space, but is heat that
must be pumped by the compressor and rejected by the
condenser.
4. The refrigerant enters the compressor at point D (the end
of the suction line) and leaves the compressor at point E.
The heat added to the compressor during the compression process is called the heat of compression. Some of
the heat of compression will be transferred to the head
of the compressor and will be rejected to the surroundings. (A fully hermetic compressor with a suction-cooled
motor would not plot out just like this. We have no way
of knowing how much heat is added to the refrigerant by
the motor, so we do not know what the temperature of
the suction gas entering the compressor cylinder would
be for a suction-cooled motor. Manufacturers obtain
their own figures for this using internal thermometers
during testing.) Notice that the line that represents the
compressor is sloped up and to the right. This indicates
that both the heat content and the pressure of the refrigerant are increasing.
5. The line that represents the compressor is drawn parallel to another set of lines on the chart that are referred
to as lines of constant entropy, Figure! 3.43. Entropy,
in our case, represents the compression process and the
relationship among the system characteristics of heat
content, absolute pressure, and absolute temperature.
These lines of constant entropy indicate that during
the compression process, the changes in pressure and
temperature are predictable. The units of constant

entropy are Btu/lb/°R, where °R is an absolute temperature (as mentioned in Unit!1 “Heat, Temperature, and
Pressure”).
6. The refrigerant leaves the compressor at point E and
contains about 144! Btu/lb. At point E, the refrigerant
is now at the outlet of the compressor and traveling in
the discharge line toward the condenser. This condenser
must reject 91!Btu/lb (144!Btu/lb 2 53!Btu/lb 5 91!Btu/
lb), called the heat of rejection. Remember that the condenser must reject all of the heat that is absorbed in the
evaporator and suction line as well as the heat generated and concentrated in the compressor during the
compression process. Therefore, the heat of rejection is
also referred to as the total heat of rejection, THOR,
Figure!3.47, because the condenser must reject all of the
heat introduced to the system.
7. At point E we can also determine the temperature at
the outlet of the compressor. We can do this by looking
at the position of the point with respect to the lines of
constant temperature, which are the downward-sloped,
curved lines on the right-hand side of the saturation
curve, Figure!3.48. The temperature of the discharge gas
is about 180°F (see the constant temperature lines for
temperature of superheated gas). When the hot gas leaves
the compressor, it contains the maximum amount of heat
that must be rejected by the condenser.
8. The refrigerant enters the condenser at point E as a
highly superheated gas. The refrigerant condensing temperature is 120°F and the hot gas leaving the compressor

Figure 3.48 The compressor discharge temperature is determined from the constant temperature lines.

is 180°F, so it contains 60°F (180°F 2 120°F 5 60°F) of
superheat. The condenser will first reduce the temperature of the discharge gas to the condensing temperature
of 120 degrees F. (This process is referred to as desuperheating.) Then, the condenser will condense the refrigerant to a liquid at 120°F and will enter the expansion
device at point A as a subcooled liquid at a temperature
of 100°F.
Other conditions may be plotted on the pressure/enthalpy
diagram. For example, suppose the head pressure of our
R-410A system increases. Keeping all other parameters of
the system constant to clarify the concept, the new system
plot is provided in Figure 3.49. With a new, higher condensing temperature of 140°F (539.1!psig or 553.8!psia), we see
the percentage of liquid leaving the expansion valve to be
about 64% (36% flash gas) with a heat content of 63!Btu/lb.
Using the same heat content leaving the evaporator, 125!Btu/
lb, we have a net refrigeration effect of 62!Btu/lb. This is a
net reduction in refrigeration effect of about 13.9% from the
original example. In addition, it can be seen that the compressor discharge temperature has increased from 180°F to
200°F. This example shows the importance of keeping systems properly maintained.
Figure!3.50 shows how increased suction line superheat
affects the first system in Figure! 3.44. The suction line has
not been insulated and absorbs heat. Notice that the amount
of sensible heat added to the refrigerant in the suction has
increased, causing an increase in the temperature of the

D

refrigerant entering the compressor. Notice the high discharge temperature (about 200°F). This is approaching the
temperature that will cause oil to break down and form acids
in the system. The compressor must pump more refrigerant to
accomplish the same refrigeration effect, and the condenser
must reject more heat.
Before its manufacturing phase-out date in 1996, R-12 used
to be a popular medium-temperature application refrigerant.
Environmental issues have caused manufacturers to explore
some different refrigerants to replace R-12, namely, R-134a
and the newer refrigerant blends. R-134a has a zero ozone
depletion potential but has oil compatibility problems and
cannot be easily retrofitted into existing R-12 systems. R-134a
also experiences a reduction in capacity when used in low-temperature applications. (Environmental issues will be covered in
Unit! 9, “Refrigerant and Oil Chemistry and Management—
Recovery, Recycling, Reclaiming, and Retrofitting.”)
A medium-temperature R-134a system, Figure 3.51,
would plot out on the pressure/enthalpy chart as follows. Use
Figure!3.52 to follow this example.
1. Refrigerant enters the expansion valve at point A at
105°F, subcooled 10° from 115°F.
2. Refrigerant leaves the expansion valve at point B with a
heat content of 47!Btu/lb. The quality is 33% vapor with
67% liquid. It then travels through the evaporator.
3. Vapor refrigerant leaves the evaporator at point C with
a heat content of 109! Btu/lb and a net refrigeration
effect of 62!Btu/lb (109 2 47 5 62).

5. The refrigerant leaves the compressor at point E with a
heat content of 130!Btu/lb. The HOC for this system is
16!Btu/lb.
6. The refrigerant is then desuperheated 45°F from point E
to the saturated vapor line.
7. The refrigerant is now gradually condensed from the
saturated vapor line to the saturated liquid line at 115°F.
This is the latent heat of condensation being rejected.
8. The saturated liquid is now subcooled from the
saturated liquid line to point A. It is subcooled 10° (115°F 2
105°F) until it enters the metering device at 105°F. The
process then repeats itself.
As mentioned earlier, R-502 was used primarily in mediumand low-temperature refrigeration applications. Its manufacture was banned in 1996. R-502 is an azeotropic refrigerant
blend with negligible temperature glide, and it behaves like a
pure compound. R-404A is its long-term replacement refrigerant and has a slightly higher working pressure than R-502.
Figure! 3.53 shows a low-temperature refrigeration system
plotted on a pressure/enthalpy chart incorporating R-404A as
the refrigerant. The system is a commercial freezer operating
with an evaporating temperature of 220°F (16.7!psig) and a
115°F (290!psig) condensing temperature.
Follow the sequence below.
1. The refrigerant enters the expansion valve at point A
at 105°F, subcooled 10°F from the 115°F condensing
temperature.
2. The refrigerant leaves the expansion valve at point B
at 220°F (55% vapor and 45% liquid) to be totally


---

## PDF Page 72

72

Section 1 Theory of Heat

Figure 3.52 An R-134a pressure/enthalpy diagram. Courtesy E. I. DuPont

1058F
A

E

C

D

B

47

evaporated in the evaporator. At point C, the refrigerant leaves the evaporator as 100% vapor with 10°F of
evaporator superheat and at a temperature of 210°F.
The difference in enthalpy between point B and point C
is the net refrigeration effect.
3. The superheated vapor enters the compressor at point D
with 30°F of total superheat and at a temperature of
10°F. The superheated vapor is now compressed to 170°F
along the line to point E.
4. The superheated vapor leaves the compressor at
point E. The refrigerant is now desuperheated from point
E to the saturated vapor line.
5. The now saturated refrigerant is gradually condensed
from the saturated vapor line to the saturated liquid line
at 115°F. This is referred to as rejection of the latent heat
of condensation.
6. The saturated liquid is subcooled 10°F (115°F 2 105°F)
from the saturated liquid line to point A, where it enters the
metering device at 105°F. The process then repeats itself.

109

#### 3.21 Plotting The
#### Refrigeration Cycle For
#### Blends With Noticeable
#### Temperature Glide
#### (Zeotropic Blends)
Temperature glide occurs when the refrigerant blend has
many temperatures as it evaporates or condenses at a given
pressure. The pressure/enthalpy diagram for refrigerants with
temperature glide differs from that for refrigerants that do
not have temperature glide. Figure!3.54 illustrates a skeletal
pressure/enthalpy diagram for a refrigerant blend with temperature glide. Notice that the lines of constant temperature
(isotherms) are not horizontal, but are angled downward
as they travel from saturated liquid to saturated vapor. As
you follow the lines of constant pressure (isobars) straight
across from saturated liquid to saturated vapor, more than


---

## PDF Page 73

### Unit 3 Refrigeration and Refrigerants

73

Figure 3.53 An R-404A low-temperature refrigeration system. Courtesy E. I. DuPont
A

E

C
B

one isotherm will be intersected. Thus, for any one pressure
(evaporating or condensing), there will be a range of associated temperatures—a temperature glide. For example, in
Figure! 3.54, for a constant pressure of 130! psia, the temperature glide is 10°F (70°F 2 60°F). The isobar of 130!psia
intersects both the 60°F and the 70°F isobars as it travels
from saturated liquid to saturated vapor.
Figure! 3.55 illustrates an actual pressure/enthalpy diagram for refrigerant R-407C, a zeotropic refrigerant blend
with very similar properties to those of R-22 in air-conditioning equipment. It is a blend of R-32, R-125, and R-134a
with a fairly large temperature glide (10°F) in the ranges of
air-conditioning applications. Notice the isotherms angled
downward from left to right, showing temperature glide.
The terms near-azeotropic and zeotropic refrigerant
blends are used in the HVACR industry because they

D

describe temperature glide and fractionation. Azeotropic
blends have negligible franctionation and temperature glide,
near-azeotropic blends have some fractionation and temperature glide, while zeotropic blends have the most fractionation and temperature glide. Refer to Unit! 9, “Refrigerant
and Oil Chemistry and Management—Recovery, Recycling,
Reclaiming, and Retrofitting,” for more in-depth information on refrigerants, refrigerant blends, temperature glide,
and fractionation. Charging methods for refrigerant blends
will be covered in Unit! 10, “System Charging.”
#### SAFETY
PRECAUTION: All refrigerants that have been discussed in
this text are stored in pressurized containers and should be
handled with care. Consult your instructor or supervisor about
the use and handling of these refrigerants. Goggles and gloves
should be worn while transferring the refrigerants from the
container to the system.-

## PDF Page 78

### UNIT 4
#### General Safety Practices
#### O B J E C T I V E S
After studying this unit, you should be able to
explain the importance of being aware of your surroundings at
all times.
● explain the importance of personal protective equipment (PPE)
and provide six examples of such equipment.
● describe and demonstrate proper procedures for working with
and around pressurized vessels and systems.
● describe the dangers associated with working on and around
electricity.
● explain what precautions a technician should take when
working with or around electricity.
● explain the importance of knowing proper first aid procedures.
● describe and demonstrate the proper use and setup of two
commonly used types of ladders.
● explain the dangers associated with exposure to extreme
temperatures and what can be done to reduce the potential
for injury resulting from these dangers.
● describe three common classifications used to describe fire
extinguishers.
● explain the acronym PASS as it refers to fire extinguisher use.
● explain the dangers associated with working on or around
rotating machinery.
● describe and demonstrate proper procedures for lifting and/or
moving heavy objects.
● explain the importance of proper ventilation as it applies to
remaining safe on the job.
● define the term “confined spaces” and the dangers associated
with them.
● explain what precautions a technician should take when
working with or around chemicals.
●

The heating, air-conditioning, and refrigeration technician is
often subjected to many dangerous conditions and situations.
Exposure to chemicals, pressurized liquids and gases, electricity, rotating machinery, and excessive heat and cold all have
the potential to cause severe personal injury or death. In addition, other physical injuries can result from improper lifting
techniques, falling objects, tripping, inadequate ventilation,
or improper use of tools and equipment. Each and every job
must be completed in a manner that ensures the safety of
both the workers on the job and the general population.
This unit describes some general safety practices and
procedures with which all technicians should be familiar.
Whether in a school laboratory, a shop, a commercial service

shop, or a manufacturing plant, technicians must always be
familiar with the location of emergency exits, first aid supplies, automatic external defibrillator (AED) equipment, and
eyewash stations. When you are on the job, be aware of how
you would get out of a building or particular location should
an emergency occur. Always use common sense and be prepared to act in the event an emergency arises.
All tools of the trade should be used for their intended
purposes and their limitations should never be exceeded.
The technician should read all manufacturer’s literature to
ensure the tool is used properly. Improper use of tools can
cause property damage and personal injury.
Safety goggles are one of the most overlooked pieces of
personal protection equipment (PPE). Flying debris can cause
an eye injury before a person can react, so it is good practice
to wear goggles anytime the possibility of injury exists. Even
when good work practices are followed, accidents do happen.
It is an excellent suggestion for every technician to take a class
on how to perform cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) and
how to properly use an automatic external defibrillator (AED).
Classes are offered by many institutions including schools, fire
departments, police stations, and the American Red Cross.
Technicians should always act professionally on the job.
Countless mistakes have been made while playing around or
otherwise not paying attention to the task at hand. Carelessness is a major cause for job site accidents and injuries.

#### 4.1 Workplace Surroundings
#### And Personal Protective
#### Equipment (Ppe)
Remaining safe on the job should be the number one concern
of any individual working in the HVACR industry. Although
it is not always possible to avoid dangerous conditions while
working in the field, there are some things that can be done
to help reduce the possibility of sustaining an injury while
working. One thing that should always be done by field personnel when arriving on a jobsite is to evaluate the workplace
conditions and identify any potentially hazardous conditions
or situations. From loose handrails to low overhangs to trip
hazards, knowing what dangerous conditions are present in
your immediate surroundings will help you avoid them as
you proceed to complete the task at hand.

78


---

## PDF Page 79

79

### Unit 4 General Safety Practices

Another thing that can be done to help prevent workplace
injuries is to dress appropriately and wear personal protective equipment (PPE) as required. Personal protection equipment is a global term used to describe the various pieces of
clothing and equipment that individuals wear to protect their
bodies in the event an accident occurs. Depending on the jobs
being performed, the recommended and/or required pieces of
PPE will vary. Typical personal protective items include, but
are definitely not limited to:
●
●
●
●
●
●
●
●
●
●
●

Figure 4.1 A refrigerant cylinder with a protective cap.! Photo by Bill
Johnson

Safety glasses or goggles
Ear plugs or muffs
Safety gloves
Work boots
Breathing masks or respirators
Hard hats
Long-sleeved shirts
Face shields
Reflective clothing
Heat-resistant creams
Sunblock

Throughout this Unit, many different types of PPE will be
discussed. It is everyone’s responsibility to work in a safe and
professional manner to help ensure that accidents, and their
resulting injuries, do not occur.

#### 4.2 Pressurized Vessels,
#### Piping, And Systems
Pressurized vessels, piping, and systems are encountered by
refrigeration and air-conditioning technicians on a daily basis.
For example, a cylinder of R-410A refrigerant in the back of
a service truck with the sun shining on it may very well be at
a temperature of 110°F on a summer day. The temperature/
pressure chart indicates that the pressure inside the cylinder at
110° is 368!psig. This reading means that the cylinder has a
pressure of 368!lb for each square inch of surface area. A!large
cylinder may have a total area of 1500!in2. This gives a total
inside pressure (pushing outward) of
1500!in2 3 368!psi 5 552,000!lb
This is equal to
552,000!lb 4 2000!lb/ton 5 276!tons
As long as the cylinder is protected and not damaged, the
pressure is well contained and will be safe. Cylinders should
not be permitted to fall over and should never be dropped.
Make certain that all tanks are properly secured at all times.
SAFETY PRECAUTION: Move the cylinder only while a protective cap is on it, if it is designed for one, Figure!4.1. ● Cylinders
too large to be carried should only be moved when chained
to an approved cart, Figure!4.2. If a refrigerant cylinder ever
falls off the moving cart, the protective cap will protect the
valve from breaking off and becoming a projectile. If the

Figure 4.2 Pressurized cylinders should be chained to and moved
safely on an approved cart.
#### Protective Cap
#### CYLINDER

#### CHAIN
#### Approved Cart

cylinder falls accidentally, the protective cap will also prevent the vaporization of all the liquid in the cylinder, which
can displace the air we breathe and cause death or personal
injury. The air is displaced because the refrigerant vapor is
denser than the air.
The pressure in a cylinder can be thought of as a potential
danger. It will not become dangerous unless it is allowed to
escape in an uncontrolled manner. The cylinder has a relief
valve at the top in the vapor space, Figure!4.3. If the pressure
builds up to the relief valve setting, the valve will start relieving vapor. As the vapor pressure is relieved, the liquid in the
cylinder will begin to vaporize and absorb heat from the surrounding liquid, producing a cooling effect. This will reduce
the pressure in the cylinder. Relief valves are set to open at
pressure levels that are above the worst typical operating conditions, which is often higher than 400 psig.
The refrigerant cylinder has a fusible plug made of a material with a low melting temperature. The plug will melt and


---

## PDF Page 80

80

Section 2 Safety, Tools and Equipment, and Shop Practices

Figure 4.3 Cylinder pressure relief valve.!Courtesy Ferris State
University. Photo by John Tomczyk

Figure 4.5 Safety glasses.!Photo by Eugene Silberstein

#### Cylinder Pressure
#### Relief Valve

blow out if the cylinder gets too hot. This prevents the cylinder
from bursting and injuring people and damaging property
around it. SAFETY PRECAUTION: Refrigerant cylinders should
be stored and transported in the upright position to keep the pressure
relief valve in contact with the vapor space, not the liquid inside the
cylinder. Some technicians may apply heat to refrigerant cylinders
while charging a system to keep the pressure from dropping in the
cylinder. This is an extremely dangerous practice. It is recommended
for the above purpose that the cylinder of refrigerant be set in a
container of warm water with a temperature no higher than 90°F. ●
You will be taking pressure readings of refrigeration and
air-conditioning systems. Liquid R-410A boils at 261°F
when it is released to the atmosphere. If you are careless and
get this refrigerant on your skin or in your eyes, it will quickly
cause frostbite. Keep your skin and eyes away from any liquid
refrigerant. When you attach gauges and take refrigerant pressure readings or transfer refrigerant into or out of a system,
wear gloves Figure!4.4, and protective eye wear, Figure 4.5.
Figure 4.4 Safety gloves to protect the hands from excessive refrigerant
temperatures.!Photo by Eugene Silberstein

SAFETY PRECAUTION: If you are working in an indoor space
and a refrigerant leak causes large amounts of refrigerant to be
released, the best thing to do is vacate the area immediately. Only
return to the area once it has been completely ventilated and it is
deemed safe to re-enter the area. ●
Released refrigerant, and any particles that may become
airborne with the blast of vapor, can harm the eyes. Wear
approved goggles, which are vented to keep them from fogging
over with condensation and which help keep the operator cool.
SAFETY PRECAUTION: All cylinders into which refrigerant is
transferred should be approved by the Department of Transportation as recovery cylinders. Figure!4.6 shows cylinders with color
codes that indicate they are approved as recovery cylinders—gray
bodies with yellow tops. It is against the law to transport refrigerant in cylinders that are not approved for this purpose. ●
In addition to the pressure potential inside a refrigerant
cylinder, there is tremendous pressure potential inside nitrogen and oxygen cylinders. They are shipped at pressures in the
range of 2500!psig and must not be moved unless the protective cap is in place. They should be chained to and moved
on carts designed for the purpose, as shown previously in
Figure! 4.2. Dropping a cylinder without the protective cap
may break the valve off the cylinder, and the pressure inside
can propel the cylinder into the air, Figure!4.7.
Nitrogen must have its pressure regulated before it can
be used because the pressure in the cylinder is too great to
be connected to a system. Nitrogen pressure is regulated
with a nitrogen pressure regulator, Figure 4.8. If a person
allowed nitrogen under cylinder pressure to enter a refrigeration system, some weak point in the system could burst,
potentially causing severe personal injury. Figure!4.8 shows
a nitrogen tank pressure of approximately 2200! psi being
regulated down to about 130! psi. Notice the downstream
safety pressure relief valve that protects the system and the
operator.

Oxygen is often used with acetylene. Acetylene cylinders
are not under the same high pressure as nitrogen and oxygen
but must be treated with the same respect because acetylene is
highly explosive. A pressure regulator must be used, Figure!4.9.
Always use an approved cart, chain the cylinder to the cart,

#### REGULATOR

#### Valve Broken Off

Oxygen also must be regulated because of high pressure. In addition, all oxygen lines must be kept absolutely
oil free. Oil residue in an oxygen regulator connection may
cause an explosion, which may blow the regulator apart in
your hand. SAFETY PRECAUTION: Oxygen is under a great
deal of pressure but should never be used as a pressure source to
pressurize lines or systems. If there is oil or oil residue in the system,
adding oxygen to pressurize them can lead to an explosion. ●

where it is supposed to function, there is nothing to fear. When
uncontrolled electrical flow occurs, if you were to touch two live
wires, for example, you are very likely to get injured.
SAFETY PRECAUTION: Electrical power should always be shut
off at the distribution panel and locked out in an approved manner
when installing equipment. The Occupational Safety and Health
Administration (OSHA) provides specific details for ensuring safe
working conditions, including electrical lockout and tagout procedures. Whenever possible, the power should be shut off and locked
out when servicing the equipment. Electrical panels have a place for
a padlock for the purpose of lockout, Figure!4.11. To avoid someone turning the power on, you should keep the panel locked when
you are out of sight and keep the only key with you at all times. Emergency STOP controls can be installed for added safety, Figure!4.12.
Do not ever think that you are good enough or smart enough to
work with live electrical power when it is not necessary. ●
However, at certain times, tests have to be made with the
circuit power on. Extreme care should be taken when making
these tests. Ensure that your hands touch only the meter probes
and that your arms and the rest of your body stay clear of all electrical terminals and connections. Always work on electric circuits as if they are energized, even if you know that they are not.

Figure 4.11 Electrical disconnect “locks out” with padlock.!Courtesy of
Ferris State University. Photo by John Tomczyk

(B)

and secure the protective cap. All stored cylinders! must be
supported so they will not fall over, and they must be separated as required by code, Figures 4.10(A) and (B).

#### 4.3 Electrical Safety
Electrical shocks and burns are ever-present hazards. It is impossible to troubleshoot all circuits with the power off, so you must
learn safe methods for troubleshooting “live” circuits. As long
as electricity is contained in the conductors and the device is


---

## PDF Page 83

83

### Unit 4 General Safety Practices
Figure 4.12 Emergency stop disconnect for electrical safety.!Courtesy
of Ferris State University. Photo by John Tomczyk

Working on electric circuits with the same degree of care all the
time will help ensure that you remain safe on the job at all times.
SAFETY PRECAUTION: Do not stand in a wet or damp area
when working on equipment. Use only the proper pieces of test
instrumentation and make certain that they are in good working condition. Wear heavy shoes with an insulating sole and heel.
Intelligent and competent technicians take all precautions. ●

#### Electrical Shock
An electrical shock occurs when you become part of an electric circuit and current flows though your body. Current
flowing through your body can cause damage to your
heart—stop it from pumping—resulting in death if it is not
restarted quickly. It is a good idea to take a comprehensive
first aid course that includes instruction on various lifesaving techniques. SAFETY PRECAUTION: To prevent electrical
shock, avoid becoming a conductor between two live wires or a
live wire and ground. ● In order for electric current to flow,
there must be a complete path for the current to take. The
amount of current flow through a circuit is directly related
to the amount of voltage being supplied to the circuit. The
higher the voltage that you are working with, the greater the
potential for injury. In this industry, we work with circuits
that may have voltages of 460!V or up to 560!V. Always know
the power supply you are working with and act accordingly.
Do not let your body create a conductive path for current to
take. Figure 4.13 illustrates a situation where a technician
has become part of a circuit.
SAFETY PRECAUTION: Technicians should use only properly
grounded power tools connected to properly grounded power
supplies and circuits. ● Caution should be used when using
portable electric tools. Although not common, some portable
electric tools are constructed with metal frames. These should
all have a grounding wire in the power cord to protect the

Figure 4.13 This figure shows ways that the technician can become part of the electrical circuit and receive an electrical shock.

(A)
operator. The tool will work without this ground wire, but
it is not safe and it is never a good idea to use tools that are
not properly grounded. If the tool develops a loose electrical
connection and the frame of the tool becomes electrified, the
ground circuit, rather than your body, will carry the current,
and a fuse or breaker will interrupt the circuit, Figure!4.14.
In some instances, technicians may use the three- to twoprong adapters at job sites because the wall receptacle may
have only two connections and their portable electric tool has
a three-wire plug, Figure! 4.15. The adapter has a third wire
that must be securely connected to a ground for the circuit to
provide protection. If this wire is fastened under the wall plate
screw and the screw terminates in an ungrounded box nailed
to a wooden wall, there is no ground protection, Figure!4.16.
Before using any plug-in tool, it is important to make certain
that the third or ground wire is properly connected to a ground.
Advances in battery and instrument technologies have
led to a dramatic increase in the popularity of plastic-cased,
battery-operated tools, Figure 4.17. Plastic-cased tools have
the motor and electrical connections insulated within the
tool. Battery-operated tools use rechargeable batteries, so it
is a good idea to have multiple charged backup batteries on
hand, Figure 4.18.

(B)
Figure 4.15 A three- to two-prong adapter.
#### Green Wire

The extension cord in Figure!4.19 is plugged into a ground
fault circuit interrupter (GFCI) receptacle. GFCIs are recommended for use with corded, portable electric tools. Designed to
help protect the operator from shock, GFCIs detect very small
electrical leaks to ground. When such an electrical current leak is
detected, the GFCI will open the circuit, preventing further current flow and greatly reducing the possibility of personal injury.

#### Electrical Burns
Do not wear metallic jewelry while working on live electric circuits because they can cause shock and possible burns. Never
use a screwdriver or other tool in an electrical panel when the

if the screwdriver slips while you were working in a panel or on

Section 2 Safety, Tools and Equipment, and Shop Practices

#### 4.4 First Aid

Figure 4.20 Contents of a typical first aid kit.! Courtesy of Ferris State
University. Photo by John Tomczyk

As the name implies, first aid is the aid that is given first,
often within moments of an individual becoming injured.
This initial assistance is often performed by non-medical
professionals in an attempt to stabilize the injured person
and prevent an injury from getting worse while waiting for
medical professionals to arrive at the scene. The first aid that
is administered, or not administered, within the first few
minutes of sustaining and injury can, quite literally, be the
difference between life and death. Common first aid practices include, but are definitely not limited to, cleaning cuts
or scratches, removing splinters, lancing blisters, applying
cream to a burn, providing fluids, and applying bandages or
other wound coverings. The main goals of first aid can be
summarized as helping to:
●
●
●
●
●

preserve life
prevent injuries from getting worse
relieve pain
promote recovery
protect unconscious individuals from further danger

When tending to an injured person that might be in
severe danger, it is important to make certain that someone
calls 9-1-1 for professional assistance. Quite often, bystanders assume that someone else has already notified the first
responders, and do not make the call. This can delay the
response time and can put the injured individual in unnecessary, additional danger. It is far better for the first responders
to receive multiple calls for help than none at all.
In order to perform first aid procedures, it is strongly recommended that a well-equipped first aid kit, Figure 4.20, be available on the job site. A well-equipped first aid kit should contain:
●
●
●
●
●
●
●
●
●
●
●
●
●
●
●
●
●
●

Adhesive bandages—various sizes
Sterile gauze pads—various sizes
Triangular bandages
Disposable latex or rubber gloves
Eye wash
Eye bath
Antiseptic pads
Antiseptic cream
Burn cream
Aspirin
Painkillers such as ibuprofen
Scissors
Tweezers
Oral thermometer
Adhesive cloth bandage tape
Ace bandages
Breathing barrier
First aid instruction booklet

To ensure that the first aid kit is complete and ready for
use when needed, it is recommended that the first aid kit be

checked regularly so that any items that have been removed
can be replaced. Please visit the OSHA and the American
Red Cross websites, www.osha.gov and www.redcross.org,
for more detailed information on first aid regulations, procedures, training, and supplies.
Not only is it a good idea to know how to properly administer traditional first aid to those in need, lives can be saved by
learning how to perform cardiopulmonary resuscitation, CPR,
and how to use an automatic external defibrillator (AED).
CPR is a lifesaving technique that is used to keep oxygenated blood flowing to the brain, and involves a combination of chest compressions and rescue breaths. Together,
these actions can help prevent brain damage or death. CPR
is performed on individuals who have stopped breathing or
whose heart has stopped pumping. Individuals should be
trained to properly perform CPR and should be retrained
every few years.
Automatic external defibrillators, AEDs, Figure 4.21, are
devices that can be used to help save the lives of individuals
who go into cardiac arrest, which is the sudden loss of heart
function. These devices deliver electric shocks, as required,
to help restore heart function. AEDs are becoming very common in public places and, although it is a good idea to receive
training on how to use the device, untrained individuals can
use them in case of an emergency. Using the device is not complicated and detailed instructions are provided on the device
itself. In addition, the AED uses voice prompts to guide users
through the process of administering the shocks as needed.

### Unit 4 General Safety Practices
Figure 4.21 An automatic external defibrillator (AED).!Photo by Eugene
Silberstein

87

Figure 4.22 An extension ladder, left, and an A-frame ladder, right.!
Photo by Jason Obrzut

#### 4.5 Ladder Safety
HVACR service technicians and installers will have to use ladders to access equipment, filters, rooftops, and system components that are located out of arm’s reach. When ladders are
needed on the job, it is good field practice to use ladders that are
made of a nonconductive material such as wood or fiberglass.
Ladders made of wood are not nearly as common now as they
once were. Ladders made of aluminum, although much lighter
than those made of wood or fiberglass, should be avoided as
they conduct electricity and can cause severe personal injury.
Two common types of ladder are the “A-frame” ladder
and the extension ladder, Figure 4.22. The A-frame ladder
is freestanding ladder, while the extension ladder is designed
to be leaned up against the side of a building or other solid
landing surface such as a roof hatch.
Before using any ladder, it is important to thoroughly
inspect it for any physical damage. If any damage is found,
the ladder should not be used. In addition, the ladder must be
free of any oil or grease and the rubber feet must be in good
shape. A-frame ladders are to be used only when fully opened
and should never be used by leaning the closed ladder up
against a wall or other object.
SAFETY PRECAUTION: A technician may raise a ladder into a
power line or place it against a live electrical hazard without realizing it. Chances should not be taken. When the technician is standing
on a nonconducting ladder, it will provide protection from electrical
shock to ground. However, it will not provide protection if the user
makes contact between two or more electrical conductors. ●

When an extension ladder is used for access to an upper
landing area such as a roof, it should extend at least 3! ft
above the landing surface. Also, the horizontal distance from
building to the foot of the ladder should be approximately
one-fourth the working length of the ladder, Figure 4.23.
Consider the example where an extension ladder must be
used to access the roof of a 20-ft building. The foot of the
ladder should be slightly more than 5 ft from the building.
In this example, the actual distance can be calculated using
the Pythagorean Theorem. It is left as a student exercise to
determine that the actual distance from the foot of the ladder to the base of the building is 5.25 ft. Ensure that ladders
are placed on a stable, level surface, not on slippery surfaces,
and that they have slip-resistant feet. Ladders should be used
only for their designed purpose and should not be loaded
with more weight than they were designed to hold. Use other
methods for raising loads to the roof when the weight is
excessive. Ladders should be maintained free of oil, grease,
and any other slipping hazards.
Working from heights on rooftops or from ladders is potentially very dangerous. Rooftops have different pitches or slants.
When the angle becomes steep, the technician should use a


---

## PDF Page 88

88

Section 2 Safety, Tools and Equipment, and Shop Practices

Figure 4.23 The side rails of a portable ladder should extend 3 ft above
the upper landing surface. The angle of the ladder should be such that
the horizontal space at the bottom is about one-fourth the distance of
the working length of the ladder.

technicians to carry cylinders up a ladder, while still maintaining three points of contact with the ladder. If three points of
contact cannot be maintained, tools and equipment should be
lifted to roof by using ropes or other acceptable methods.

#### WORKING

OF
#### LADDER

The use of heat requires special care. Torches used for soldering, brazing, welding, among other things, have a high
concentration of heat. Combustible materials may be in the
area where torches are used. When soldering or using concentrated heat, you should have a fire extinguisher close by,
and you should know exactly where it is and how to use
it, Figure!4.25. Learn to use a fire extinguisher before a fire
occurs. A fire extinguisher should always be included as a
part of the service tools and equipment on a service truck.
Although there are many different types of fire extinguishers available, they all work in the same way. Fire extinguisher use can best be described by the acronym PASS.
●

●

●

safety belt and be tied off in such a manner that a fall would be
stopped. Technicians should always work in pairs when doing
rooftop work or when working on a high ladder. In many cases,
the ladder should be secured at the top, particularly if it is to be
used for several climbs. SAFETY PRECAUTION: Never use the
cross-bracing on the far side of stepladders for climbing. The far side is
only safe for climbing if it is provided with steps. ●
When climbing ladders, you should always be making three
points of contact with the ladder. These three contact points
can be two feet and one hand, or two hands and one foot.
Therefore, when carrying tools and equipment up a ladder, care
must be taken to use the proper techniques, methods, or tools.
Figure!4.24 shows a service technician transporting a refrigerant cylinder with a carrying strap. The carrying strap enables

●

P: Pull the pin from the top of the fire extinguisher. Without removing the pin, the handle cannot be squeezed.
A: Aim the nozzle of the fire extinguisher at the BASE of the
fire. The nozzle should be about 6 to 8 feet from the fire.
S: Squeeze the handle on the fire extinguisher.
S: Sweep the nozzle from side to side until the fire has been
extinguished.

safely.!Courtesy SC Products, Inc. Wilmington, DE

Figure 4.26 Fire extinguisher details showing the types of fires it should

Figure 4.27 A shield used when brazing or soldering.

#### Extinguisher Markings

#### ORDINARY

#### (GREEN)
