#### (BLUE)

Many fire extinguishers are designed for specific types of
fires. Type A is for trash, wood, and paper fires. Type B is for
burning liquids and Type C is for electrical equipment fires.
However, some fire extinguishers are rated for all three types
of fires, Figure!4.26. Usually, these extinguishers are the drychemical type. Make sure you know what type of fire extinguisher you are using on a fire, or the fire can spread and
serious personal injury and property damage can occur.
SAFETY PRECAUTION: When a solder connection must be
made next to combustible materials or a finished surface, use a
shield of noncombustible material for insulation, Figure! 4.27.
A! fire-resistant spray may also be used to decrease the flammability of wood if a torch must be used nearby. The spray retardant
should be used with an appropriate shield. ● A shield is also
often necessary when soldering within an equipment cabinet,
for example, when an ice-maker compressor is changed or
when a drier must be soldered in line. A shield should be used
to protect any wires that may be close by.
SAFETY PRECAUTION: Never solder tubing lines that are
sealed. Service valves or Schrader ports should be open before soldering is attempted. When heat from a torch is added to a sealed
tubing line, pressure will build up inside the tubing and may result
in serious personal injury. ●

SAFETY PRECAUTION: Hot refrigerant lines, hot heat exchangers, and hot motors can burn your skin and leave a permanent scar. Care should be used while handling them. ●
SAFETY PRECAUTION: Working in very hot weather or in hot
attics can be very hard on the body. Technicians should be aware
of how their body and the bodies of those around them are reacting to the working conditions. Watch for signs of overheating, such
as someone’s face turning very red or someone who has stopped
sweating. Get them out of the heat and cool them off. Overheating
can be life-threatening; call for emergency help. ●
In warmer climates, work in attics should be performed,
whenever possible, in the early morning hours, before the
heat of the day creates unsafe working conditions.
When exposed to excessive heat, the body reacts by exhibiting some tell-tale signs that something is wrong. Under no circumstances should these signs be ignored. If ignored, severe
personal injury can result. Two common ailments related to
excessive heat exposure are heat exhaustion and heatstroke.
The symptoms for heat exhaustion and heat stroke are provided in Figure 4.28.
If it is suspected that an individual is suffering from
heat exhaustion, move the victim to a cooler place and
encourage the individual to drink cool water. Applying cool
Figure 4.28 Symptoms of heat exhaustion and heat stroke.
#### Heat Exhaustion

#### HEATSTROKE

Feeling faint or dizzy

Throbbing headache

Excessive sweating

No sweating

Skin is cool, pale and clammy

Skin is hot, red and dry

Nauseous feeling, vomiting
Rapid, weak pulse

Rapid, strong pulse

Muscle cramps and weakness
may occur

Confusion, slurred speech,
loss of consciousness

Core body temperature:
Normal to 1048F

Core body temperature:
Above 1048F

## PDF Page 90

90

Section 2 Safety, Tools and Equipment, and Shop Practices

compresses and taking a cold shower will help reduce the
symptoms. If left untreated, heat exhaustion can become
worse, possibly resulting in heatstroke, which can be
life-threatening.
If heat stroke is suspected, it is important to call 9-1-1
immediately. If possible, move the individual to a cool place
and, using a cool, damp rag, apply cool compresses to the
skin to help further cool the victim.
To avoid becoming a victim of heat-related injuries, technicians should:
●

●
●
●

●

Figure 4.29 Never wear a necktie or loose clothing when using or
working around rotating equipment.

Attempt to stay indoors as much as possible during the
hottest part of the day
Drink water throughout the day, even if they are not thirsty
Wear loose-fitting, light-colored clothing whenever possible
Avoid diuretics, or drinks that dehydrate, such as coffee
and other caffeinated products
Wear “breathable” fabrics, such as cotton

#### 4.7 Cold
Cold can be as harmful as heat. Liquid refrigerant can freeze
your skin or eyes instantaneously. But long exposure to cold
is also harmful. Working in cold weather can cause frostbite.
Wear proper clothing and waterproof boots, which also help
protect against electrical shock. When cold, wet, and uncomfortable, technicians do not always think clearly and might
make decisions that can put them in danger. Make it a point
to stay warm. SAFETY PRECAUTION: Be aware of the windchill when working outside. The wind can quickly take the heat out
of your body, and frostbite can occur. Waterproof boots not only
protect your feet from water and cold but help to protect you from
electrical shock when you are working in wet weather. However, do
not depend on these boots for protection against electrical hazards.
Low-temperature freezers are just as cold in the middle of the
summer as they are in the winter. Cold-weather gear must be used
when working inside these freezers. ● For example, an expansion valve may need changing, and you may be in the freezer
for more than an hour. It is a shock to your body when you
step out from a zero-degree freezer to the warm space outside, which may be as hot as 100°F. If you are servicing any
low-temperature applications, carry a coat and gloves with
you and be sure to wear them as needed.

SAFETY PRECAUTION: When starting an open motor, stand
well to the side of the motor drive mechanism. ● If the coupling
or belt were to fly off the drive, it would fly outward in
the!direction of rotation of the motor. All set screws or holding mechanisms must be tight before a motor is started, even
if the motor is not connected to a load. Keep all wrenches
away from a coupling or pulley. A wrench or nut thrown
from a coupling can be a lethal projectile.
SAFETY PRECAUTION: When a large motor, such as a fan motor, is coasting to a stop, do not try to stop it. If you try to stop the motor
and fan by gripping the belts, the momentum of the fan and motor
may pull your hand into the pulley and under the belt, Figure!4.30. ●
SAFETY PRECAUTION: Never wear jewelry while working on
a job that requires much movement. A ring may be caught on a
nail head, or a bracelet may be caught on the tailgate of a truck as
you jump down. ●

Figure 4.30 Never attempt to stop a motor or other mechanism by
gripping the belt.

#### 4.8 Mechanical Equipment
Rotating equipment can damage property and cause severe
personal injury. Motors that drive fans, compressors, and
pumps are among the most dangerous because they have
so much power.
SAFETY PRECAUTION: If a shirt sleeve or
coat were caught in a motor drive pulley or coupling, severe injury
could occur. Loose clothing should never be worn around rotating machinery, Figure!4.29. Even a small electric drill can wind a
necktie up before the drill can be shut off. ●

### Unit 4 General Safety Practices
Figure 4.31 Use a face shield when grinding.!Photo by Eugene
Silberstein

Figure 4.33 (A) Use your legs, not your back, to lift objects. Keep your
back straight. (B) Use a back brace belt.!Courtesy Wagner Products Corp.

(A)

Figure 4.32 Keep the tool rest on a grinder adjusted properly.

10
(9 mm) SPACE
16

SAFETY PRECAUTION: When using a grinder to sharpen
tools, remove burrs, or for other reasons, always use a face
shield, Figure! 4.31.● Most grinding stones are made
for grinding ferrous metals, such as cast iron, steel, and
stainless steel. However, other stones are made for nonferrous metals, such as aluminum, copper, or brass. Use
the correct grinding stone for the metal you are grinding.
The tool rest should be adjusted to approximately 1/16!in.
from the grinding stone, Figure! 4.32. As the stone wears
down, keep the tool rest adjusted to this setting. A grinding stone must not be used on a grinder that turns faster
than the stone’s rated maximum revolutions per minute
(rpm), as it may explode.

(B)

SAFETY PRECAUTION: When you must lift, use your legs, not
your back, and wear an approved back brace belt, Figure!4.33. ●
Some available tools are a hand truck, a lift gate on a pickup
truck, and a portable dolly, Figure!4.34.
When moving large equipment across a carpeted or tiled
floor or across a gravel-coated roof, first lay down some plywood. Keep the plywood in front of the equipment as it is
moved along. When equipment has a flat bottom, such as a
package air conditioner, short lengths of pipe may be used to
move the equipment across a solid floor, Figure!4.35.

#### 4.10 Refrigerants In Your
#### Breathing Space
Refrigerant vapors and many other gases are heavier than
air and can displace the oxygen in a closed space. Because
of this, proper ventilation must be used at all times to avoid
being overcome by lack of oxygen. Should you be in a confined space and the concentration of refrigerant become too
great, you may not notice it until it is too late. Your symptoms would be a dizzy feeling, and your lips may become
numb. If you should feel this way, move quickly to a place
with fresh air.

#### 4.9 Moving Heavy Objects

SAFETY PRECAUTION: Proper ventilation should be set up in
advance of starting a job. Fans may be used to push or pull fresh
air into a confined space where work must be performed. Crossventilation can help prevent a buildup of fumes. ●

From time to time heavy objects must be moved. Think out
the best and safest method for moving these objects. Never
rely solely on muscle power. Special tools can help you move
large and heavy equipment safely. Do not try to lift heavy
equipment by yourself. Get help from another person and
use tools and equipment designed for that purpose.

SAFETY PRECAUTION Confined spaces can be dangerous and
the utmost care should be taken when working in them. A confined
space is an area that has limited access and is, by Occupational
Safety and Health Administration (OSHA) standards, not suitable
for human inhabitants. Confined spaces have the potential to pose
many hazards including injury due to the inhalation of refrigerants


---

## PDF Page 92

92

Section 2 Safety, Tools and Equipment, and Shop Practices

Figure 4.34 (A) A hand truck.!(B) The lift gate on a pickup truck.!(C) A dolly.!Photos by Bill Johnson

(B)

(A)

(C)
Figure 4.35 Moving equipment using short lengths of pipe as rollers.

or other substances. Other potential hazards include fires, falls,
entrapment, and flooding. Always be aware of your surroundings
and know how to react in the event an accident occurs. When working in tight spaces, make certain that you are not alone and that
others know you are there in the event something goes wrong. ●

Some installations require special leak detectors with
alarms. These detectors sound an alarm well in advance of
a harmful buildup. Figure!4.36 shows a refrigerant-specific
leak detector for HCFC-123 (R-123) that will sniff out certain parts per million (ppm) of refrigerant. If the programmed
ppm level is reached, the detector will sound an alarm and
color-coded lights will flash. When the leak-detector alarm
sounds, take proper precautions, which may require special
breathing gear. Turn on the ventilation system. Figure!4.37
shows an evacuation plan for the second floor of a building
in case of a refrigerant leak, fire, or any other emergency.
Refrigerant vapors that have been heated during soldering or because they have passed through an open flame
(e.g., when there is a leak in the presence of a fire) are
dangerous. They have a strong odor, are toxic, and may
cause harm.
SAFETY PRECAUTION: If you are soldering
in a confined place, keep your head below and to the side of the
rising fumes and make sure that you have plenty of ventilation,
Figure!4.38. This helps ensure that you do not inhale the fumes
and are also out of the way in case molten flux or solder drips
from the fitting. ●

The American National Standards Institute (ANSI) and
the American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers (ASHRAE) has developed Standard 34-2010
for Designation and Safety Classifications of Refrigerants.
Figure! 4.39 is a matrix outlining Standard 34-2010, and the
following explains its letter and number classifications.
Class A—For refrigerants where toxicity levels have not
been identified at concentrations less than or equal to
400!ppm (parts per million).
Class B—For refrigerants where evidence of toxicity
exists at concentrations below 400!ppm.
Class 1—For refrigerants that do not show flame propagation when tested in 65°F air at 14.7!psia.
Class 2—For refrigerants with a lower flammability limit
of more than 0.00625!lb/ft3 when tested at 70°F and
14.7!psia. They also have a heat of combustion of less
than 8174!Btu/lb.

A3

B3

A2

B2

A2L*

B2L*

A1

B1

#### LOWER
#### TOXICITY

#### HIGHER
#### TOXICITY

#### Increasing Toxicity
* A2L and B2L are lower flammability refrigerants with a maximum
burning velocity of #10 cm/s (3.9 in./s).

Class 3—For refrigerants with high flammability. Their
lower flammability limit is less than or equal to
0.00625! lb/ft3 when tested at 70°F and 14.7! psia.
They have a heat of combustion greater than or equal
to 8174!Btu/lb.
The two subclasses (A2L and B2L) apply to lower flammability refrigerants. The capital letter “L” refers to
“Lower” flammability.

## PDF Page 94

94

Section 2 Safety, Tools and Equipment, and Shop Practices

#### 4.11 Using Chemicals

Figure 4.41 Body wash station.! Courtesy of Ferris State University.
Photo by John Tomczyk

HVACR service technicians come in contact with many different chemicals on a daily basis while performing their job.
Chemicals are used to help perform a number of different
tasks such as cleaning dirty condenser and evaporator coils.
The chemicals that are used are typically mild, except for
some harsher products that are used for water treatment.
SAFETY PRECAUTION: These chemicals should be handled
according to the manufacturer’s directions. Do not get careless. If
you spill chemicals on your skin or splash them in your eyes, follow the manufacturer’s first aid instructions and go to a doctor.
It is a good idea to read the entire label before starting a job. It is
hard to read the first aid treatment for eyes after your eyes have
been damaged. ●
Following a compressor burnout, the refrigerant and
oil in a compressor can be harmful, as acids can form when
the compressor fails. The contaminated refrigerant and oil
may be hazardous to your skin, eyes, and lungs.
#### SAFETY
PRECAUTION: Keep your distance if a refrigerant line is subject to
rupture or any amount of refrigerant is allowed to escape. ●
Emergency eye and body wash stations should be readily
available in order to deal with accidents involving any harmful chemicals or refrigerants, Figures 4.40 and 4.41. Emergency call boxes can also be located throughout the building
for added safety, Figure 4.42.

Figure 4.40 Eye wash station.!Courtesy of Ferris State University. Photo
by John Tomczyk

Figure 4.42 Emergency call station.!Courtesy of Ferris State University.
Photo by John Tomczyk

1. To help remain safe on the job, it is always recommended
that field personnel
A. test fire extinguisher operation regularly.

D. work alone to help prevent careless accidents.
2. Which of the following represents three pieces of personal protection equipment (PPE)?
A. Ear plugs, ladders, safety gloves
B. Hard hats, ear plugs, work boots
C. Back brace, safety glasses, plastic-banded watches
D. Short-sleeved shirts, hard hats, work boots

A. Nitrogen will slowly leak out
B. There will be a loud bang
C. The tank can become airborne
D. The nitrogen vapor will freeze

service technician to frostbite?
A. Connecting or removing a service hose from a suction-line service port
B. Connecting or removing a service hose from a liquidline service port
C. Connecting or removing a service hose from a discharge line service port
D. Connecting or removing a service hose from the inlet
port on a vacuum pump

temperature relationship.

D. Using aluminum ladders will protect individuals from

describe the instrument used to measure the speed of an air

- Keep tools free from grease and oil to prevent slipping, which can
cause severe personal injury.
- Use sharp blades, bits, saws, and other cutting instruments. Sharp
blades require less force to use and, as a result, are much safer. Dull
blades, bits, and saws should not be used.
- Many tool-related accidents and injuries result from distractions. Concentrate on the task at hand to help reduce the chance of becoming
injured.
- Tools are designed to perform specific tasks. Do not use tools for tasks
or jobs that they were not intended to perform. Improper tool use
can result in personal injury.
- Inspect the cords on all power tools before using them. Do not use
corded power tools if the cord is damaged.
- Be sure to fully understand the operation of a tool before using it.
Always read all literature provided with the tool prior to use.

#### 5.1 Introduction To Tools,
#### Instrumentation, And
#### EQUIPMENT
Air-conditioning, heating, and refrigeration technicians must
know how to properly use hand tools and specialized equipment relating to this field. Technicians must be completely
aware of how a particular tool operates as well as its limitations. Technicians should read all tool-use instructions prior to
using a tool and must not operate or use the tool if its intended
use does not align with the task to be performed. Using the
97


---

## PDF Page 98

98

Section 2 Safety, Tools and Equipment, and Shop Practices

correct tool is more efficient, saves time, and is safer. Accidents
often can be prevented by using the correct tool. This unit contains a brief description of some of the general and specialized
tools and equipment that HVACR technicians use in the field.
Some of the tools and pieces of equipment introduced here are
described in more detail throughout the book, primarily in the
units where the tools are used.
Generally speaking, when purchasing tools, it is a good
idea to buy the best tools that you can afford. Good hand
tools are usually more expensive, but they typically perform
better and will usually last longer. For example, an inexpensive
screwdriver will often have an undersized handle and will be
hard to grip. Also, the bit on the end will not be made of hard,
tempered steel and will deform easily. The better choice would
be a screwdriver with a comfortable handle and a very hard
tip, which is under great stress when the technician is working
with a very tight screw. Good hand tools are usually guaranteed for replacement if they are broken during regular use.

The tip on the end of the screwdriver is inserted into the slot or
groove in the screw that needs to be turned. The most common
types of screwdriver in the United States are the Phillips head,
Figure 5.1(A) and the straight-slot, Figure!5.1(B). To gain access
to tight-fitting spaces, offset screwdrivers are readily available,
Figure 5.1(C). Other screwdriver tip configurations are shown
in Figure 5.1(D). To reduce the number of tools that must be
carried by a technician, multitip screwdriver tools, such as the
6-in-1 tool shown in Figure!5.1(E) are very popular. Some multitip tools have as many as twelve different interchangeable
tips. When selecting a screwdriver, make certain that the screwdriver tip fits snugly into the screw slot, Figure 5.2.
Figure 5.2 The screwdriver tip must fit snugly into the slot. (A) This tip
is far too small. (B) This tip is too thick to fit into the slot. (C) This tip is too
wide. (D) This tip fits perfectly into the slot.

handle grip on one end and a uniquely shaped tip on the other.

Figure 5.3 Thermostat screwdriver. Photo by Eugene Silberstein

Figure 5.5 Nut drivers are designed to turn hex-headed screws and
bolts.

Figure 5.6 Nut drivers with hollow shafts are used when the screw or
bolt extends past the nut.

In the HVACR industry, control wires must be connected
to control devices such as thermostats, so very small thermostats, referred to as thermostat screwdrivers, Figure 5.3,
should be a part of every service technician’s tool box. These
small screwdrivers often have a special tool called a Schrader
tool incorporated into the grip. The Schrader tool is used to
loosen and tighten Schrader pins, which are part of self-sealing
valves that are similar to those found in car and bicycle tires.
SAFETY PRECAUTION: Screwdrivers are intended to
tighten and loosen screws. They are not to be used as substitutes
for chisels, pry bars, or any other tool. Personal injury can result if
tools are improperly used. ●
Nut drivers, Figure 5.4, have socket heads and are used
primarily to turn hex-head screws on the cabinets of HVACR
equipment, Figure 5.5. They are available in a number of
configurations including solid shaft, hollow shaft, extra-long
and stubby shafts. The hollow-shaft variety allows the screw
to extend into the shaft when it extends past the nut, Figure!5.6. Stubby shafts are used to get into tight spaces. The
most commonly used nut driver sizes are 1/4 in. and 5/16 in.
Figure 5.4 Nut drivers. Photo by Eugene Silberstein

#### Wrenches And Sockets
#### Adjustable Wrench And Refrigeration Service
WRENCH. Two very common wrenches that can be found

in the HVACR service technician’s toolbox are the adjustable
wrench and the refrigeration service wrench, Figure 5.7. The
adjustable wrench is a desirable tool because it is a single tool
that can be used to turn many different size hex-head bolts.
When using the adjustable wrench, it is important that the
tool is positioned so the user pulls on the wrench while exerting force on the stationary portion of the tool’s jaw, Figure
5.8. The refrigeration service wrench is a ratcheting tool that
is used to turn square stems on valves and other system components, Figure!5.9. The ratchet feature on this wrench allows
the turning direction to be changed by pushing the lever-like
button near the end of the wrench. Two openings on each end
of the wrench allow it to be used on four sizes of valve stems or
fittings. Another tool that is similar to the refrigeration service
wrench is the ratcheting hex wrench, Figure 5.10. This wrench
works in exactly the same manner as the refrigeration service
wrench, but is used on hex-head bolts.

#### Open-End, Box-End, And Combination Wrenches.

Other common wrenches are the open-end, box-end, and combination wrench, Figure 5.11. The open-end wrench is desirable because it can be slipped onto a nut from the side but,
because it has an open end, can slip. The open-end wrench is
not intended to loosen extremely tight nuts or bolts or to provide the final tightening of a fastener. The box-end wrench is a
general-duty wrench but provides more torque than the openend wrench and is less likely to slip. The combination wrench
has an open-end wrench on one end and a box-end wrench on
the other.
FLARE NUT WRENCHES. The flare nut wrench, Figure 5.12,
is used like a box-end wrench. The opening at the end of the
Figure 5.9 The refrigeration service wrench is used to turn square
stems. Source: Ritchie Engineering Company, Inc. Yellow Jacket Products
Division.

Figure 5.11 Open-end wrench (left), box wrench (center), combination
wrench (right). Photo by Eugene Silberstein

### Unit 5 Tools, Instrumentation, and!Equipment
Figure 5.12 Flare nut wrenches. Photo by Bill Johnson

101

wrench allows it to be slipped over the tubing. The wrench
can then be placed over the flare nut to tighten or loosen it.
Adjustable wrenches should not be used on flare nuts because
they may round off the corners of fittings made of soft brass.
SOCKET WRENCHES. Socket wrenches are used to turn hex-

head bolts and are used in conjunction with a ratcheting handle. A socket wrench set, such as the one shown in Figure!5.13,
is comprised of a set of socket wrenches, a reversible, ratcheting
handle, at least one extension, and a universal joint socket. The
universal joint socket allows the tool to be used at an angle,
which is beneficial when access is limited or difficult.
Allen wrenches, also referred to as Allen
keys, Figure 5.14, are hex-shaped tools that are used to turn
screws and bolts that have heads fashioned with hex-shaped
sockets. To use the Allen key, the tool is inserted into the head
of the screw or bolt and then rotated to either tighten or
loosen it. Allen wrenches are commonly sold as a multikey
fold-out set, Figure 5.14(left), but can be purchased as a set of
separate keys. Traditional Allen keys must be inserted straight
into the screw, but ball-tipped Allen keys, Figure 5.14(right),
can be used at an angle. This is especially helpful when the
screw to be turned is not readily accessible. Allen wrenches
are also available as individual T-handled tools, Figure 5.15.

#### Allen Wrenches.

#### HAMMERS
There are many different types of hammers. Some of the most
commonly used types are the claw hammer, the sheet metal
hammer, the ball peen hammer, and the soft-faced hammer,
Figure 5.16. Each type is intended to perform specific tasks.
The claw hammer is the most common type of hammer. The
head of the hammer is used to drive nails and the claw portion of the tool is used to pry and remove nails from wood.
The sheet metal, or tin-knockers hammer, is typically used
by individuals who fabricate and/or install ductwork. This
hammer, often called a setting hammer, has a square, flat
face that is used for flattening seams without causing damage to the metal. The sheet metal hammer also has a beveled,
tapered peen that is used to assemble duct sections. The ball
peen hammer, often referred to as a mechanic’s hammer, has
a head that resembles the head of a traditional claw hammer

102

Section 2 Safety, Tools and Equipment, and Shop Practices

Figure 5.16 Hammers. From left to right: Claw-hammer, sheet metal
hammer, ball peen hammer, soft-faced hammer. Photo by Eugene
Silberstein

Slip-joint and pump pliers have a tongue-and-groove mechanism that, as with the general purpose pliers, allows the user
to adjust the jaws based on the job being performed. The
longer handle provides more torque than general slip-joint
pliers. These pliers are popular with plumbers and other
tradespeople who work with larger diameter pipes. Locking
pliers are the most versatile of the three types of pliers just
discussed. Locking pliers can function as a pair of pliers, a
wrench, or even a vise.

#### Electrical Pliers

and a rounded peen that resembles a ball. This rounded peen
is typically used to shape metal. Soft-faced hammers or mallets are used when the surface being hammered must not be
damaged. These hammers are often used when working with
wood or other softer materials.

#### General Purpose Pliers

Pliers and wiring tools commonly used by electricians are
available in many designs. The most common pliers used in
conjunction with electrical wiring are the side-cutting pliers,
the needle-nose pliers, and the linesman pliers, Figure 5.18.
Side-cutting pliers are typically used to cut wire or other thin
metal object close to a surface. Needle-nose pliers are used
to gain access to tight spaces. Needle-nose pliers are popular
for HVACR control wiring. Linesman pliers are heavier than
needle-nose pliers and can be used to perform a number of
functions. They can cut and strip wire as well as perform
bending and twisting operations.

#### Wiring Tools

As with other tools, there are many different types of pliers. Each type is intended to perform specific tasks, so it is
best field practice to use the appropriate pair of pliers for
the job. Three of the most common types of pliers used by
the HVACR service technician are slip-joint pliers, pump
pliers, and locking pliers, Figure 5.17. General purpose slipjoint pliers are used for a number of tasks including holding,
turning, and bending. They have a slip joint, allowing the
user to adjust the opening dimension of the jaws. These pliers
are not intended to perform the final tightening of fasteners.

The HVACR service technician needs to work with electrical
circuits and perform a number of tasks with wire. Common
wiring tools include manual wire strippers, automatic wire
strippers, and crimping tools, Figure 5.19. To use the manual
wire stripper, the wire to be stripped is inserted into the proper
slot based on the gauge of the wire. The tool is closed onto the
wire and then pulled off to remove the insulation from the wire.
Manual stripping tools can also cut wire. Automatic wire strippers are also popular. To use this tool, simply insert the wire
into the tool while the tool’s handle is not squeezed. The length
of the strip is determined by the amount of wire extending

Figure 5.17 Slip-joint pliers (left), pump pliers (center), locking pliers
(right). Photo by Eugene Silberstein

Figure 5.18 Side-cutting pliers (left), needle-nose pliers (center), linesman pliers (right). Photo by Eugene Silberstein

beyond the cutters. Hold the wire in one hand and squeeze
the handles with the other. When the handles are released, the
stripped wire can be removed. The crimping tool is a combination tool that can be used for crimping solderless connectors,
stripping wire, cutting wire, and cutting small bolts.

#### SAWS
Saws, Figure 5.20, are selected based on a number of factors
including the material being cut and the accessibility of the
item being cut. Materials that are typically cut by HVACR
field personnel are wood, drywall, and metal. Common saw
types are wood saws, keyhole saws, and hacksaws. The keyhole saw, shown second from the left in Figure 5.20, is typically used to cut sheetrock. The pointed tip on the saw allows
the user to simply push the saw into the sheetrock, preventing
any additional damage from occurring. There are also variations on saws that make it easier to get into tighter spaces.
For example, the saw in the center of Figure 5.20 is a folding

103

Figure 5.21 Hole saws. Photo by Eugene Silberstein

jab saw that accommodates the blade from a reciprocating
saw. This allows the user to cut through material by hand
with a heavy duty blade otherwise intended for use on power
tools. The rightmost saw provides the user with the ability to
use a hacksaw blade in spaces where a traditional hacksaw
might not fit.
Although not a handsaw, hole saws, Figure 5.21, are
included in this section. Hole saws are available in a wide
range of sizes and are used in conjunction with power drills.
The hole saw is mounted on an arbor, which is secured to the
drill. A pilot drill bit is mounted in the arbor. This pilot bit
allows the user to ensure hole location, while preventing the
hole saw from drifting during use.

#### Utility Hand Tools
FLASHLIGHTS AND DROPLIGHTS. HVACR field personnel
often work in dark locations, so providing light is extremely
important to ensure that a safe environment is maintained.
Battery-powered lights, Figure 5.22, or corded droplights,

TAPE MEASURES. Typical tape measures, Figure 5.24(left),

can be used, such as the 150-foot tape measure shown in
Figure 5.24(right). Digital laser distance meters can also be
used, Figure 5.25.

CLAMPS. Clamps, Figure 5.29, are tools used to temporarily
hold objects together for a variety of purposes. The clamps
shown in Figure 5.29 are referred to as C-clamps due to their
shape, but other clamp types include trigger clamps, parallel
clamps, spring clamps, and corner/angle clamps. Clamps are
available in a number of different sizes and should be selected
based on the job being performed.

FISH TAPE. A fish tape, Figure 5.31, is typically used to
snake electrical wires through passages such as walls and
electrical piping or conduit. The fish tape is passed through
the wall first and, once it has reached the end, one end of the
wire is connected to the end of the fish tape. The fish tape is
then carefully pulled out, pulling the wire behind it.

FILES. Files, Figure 5.30, are used to remove small amounts

extract sealants or adhesives from tubes that are inserted into
the gun. By squeezing the handle on the caulking gun, the bottom of the sealant or adhesive cartridge is pushed up, causing
the material to be pushed from the tube. A wide variety of
sealants and adhesives are available in tube or cartridge form
that fit into the caulking gun.

of material, usually from wood or metal. There are a number of different configurations of files including flat, round,
and half-round. When a larger amount of material is to be
removed, a rasp is used. A rasp closely resembles a file, but
they have teeth that are coarser than those on files.

CAULKING GUN. The caulking gun, Figure 5.32, is used to

SOLDERING IRONS. A soldering iron, Figure 5.35, is used
for a number of jobs including joining wires together, connecting wires to circuit boards or other components, and
“tinning” the end of a stranded conductor so that it can be
connected under a screw terminal, Figure 5.36.
Figure 5.35 Soldering iron. Photo by Eugene Silberstein.
COLD CHISELS. Cold chisels, Figure 5.33, are used to cut
through cold metals, such as screws, bolts, and nails. Cold
chisels are struck with a ball peen hammer to cut away the
desired metal. Carpenter’s claw hammers should not be used
in conjunction with cold chisels as their faces are not tempered for striking hard metals and can chip. Always use a
chisel that is slightly wider than the metal being cut.
STAPLERS. Staplers, Figure 5.34, and stapling tackers are
used to fasten insulation and other soft materials to wood;
some types may be used to secure low-voltage wiring.

Figure 5.36 A tinned wire being secured under a screw terminal. Photo
by Eugene Silberstein

Figure 5.33 Cold chisels. Photo by Eugene Silberstein

### Unit 5 Tools, Instrumentation, and!Equipment

#### 5.3 Power Tools

107

Figure 5.39 Drill bits. Photo by Eugene Silberstein

#### Electric Drills
Portable electric drills are used extensively by HVACR technicians. They are available as power-corded (115 V) tools, Figure 5.37, or as cordless, battery-powered, tools, Figure 5.38.
If the drill has a cord and is not double-insulated, it must
have a three-prong plug and be used only with a grounded
receptacle. Drills that have variable speed and reversing
options are recommended.
Many technicians prefer the battery-operated type for its
convenience and safety. Line voltage power supplies are not
required to operate battery-powered tools provided that the
batteries are kept charged. Electric drills are used for a number of purposes including the creation of holes. It is a good
idea to have a complete set of drills bits, Figure 5.39, on hand
to ensure that the proper size hole is drilled.

#### Reciprocating Saws
Reciprocating saws create a back-and-forth action to cut
through a variety of materials and are available as corded,
Figure 5.40, or battery-powered, Figure 5.41, tools. There are

Figure 5.40 Corded reciprocating saw. Photo by Eugene Silberstein

Figure 5.37 Corded drill. Courtesy of Milwaukee Electric Tool Corporation

Figure 5.38 Battery-powered drills. Photo by Eugene Silberstein

Figure 5.41 Cordless, battery-powered reciprocating saw. Photo by
Eugene Silberstein


---

## PDF Page 108

108

Section 2 Safety, Tools and Equipment, and Shop Practices

Figure 5.42 Battery-powered circular saw. Photo by Eugene Silberstein

many different types of blades available for use on these saws
and are typically classified as metal, wood, and general purpose. The number of teeth on the blade determines how fine
or rough the cut will be. The more teeth per inch, the finer the
finished cut will be. Reciprocating saws are commonly used
for demolition work.

Figure 5.44 Battery-powered jigsaw. Photo by Eugene Silberstein

Figure 5.45 Pilot hole for jigsaw blade.

#### Circular Saws
Circular saws, Figure 5.42, are typically used to make straight
cuts in wood sheets, such as plywood. This type of saw uses a
round blade. As with other blades, the number of teeth on the
blade determines how fine or rough the cut will be. The more
teeth per inch, the finer the finished cut will be. As opposed
to the reciprocating saw, that uses a back-and-forth motion,
the circular saw uses a rotational motion.

#### JIGSAWS
Jigsaws are available as both corded, Figure 5.43, and battery-powered, Figure 5.44, tools.
Figure 5.43 Corded jigsaw. Photo by Bill Johnson

Like the reciprocating saw, the jigsaw relies on a backand-forth action to perform the cutting. Unlike the reciprocating saw, the blades on a jigsaw are much thinner, making
the tool extremely useful when making rounded cuts. When
an internal cut needs to be made, a hole can first be drilled
in the material to be cut, and the jigsaw blade can then be
inserted into the hole, Figure 5.45.

#### 5.4 Electrical Test
#### INSTRUMENTS
DIGITAL MULTIMETERS (DMMs)
The digital multimeter (DMM), Figure 5.46, often referred
to as a volt-ohm-milliammeter (VOM), is a piece of electrical instrumentation that measures voltage (volts), resistance
(ohms), and current (milliamperes). There are many types
of DMMs available and many have the ability to measure


---

## PDF Page 109

### Unit 5 Tools, Instrumentation, and!Equipment

109

Figure 5.46 Digital multimeter. Courtesy of Fieldpiece Instruments.

Figure 5.48 Voltage tester. Courtesy of Fluke Corporation.

Figure 5.47 Meter test leads. Courtesy of Fieldpiece Instruments.

Sometimes, a field technician only needs to know whether
or not a particular circuit is energized. When such is the case, a
voltage tester, such as the one shown in Figure 5.48, can be used.

#### Clamp-On Ammeter
The clamp-on ammeter, Figure 5.49, is a versatile instrument
that has operable jaws that are clamped around a single conductor in an electric circuit to measure the current flow through
the conductor. The reason that this particular instrument is so
versatile is that it can also measure voltage and resistance. A
Figure 5.49 Clamp-on ammeter. Courtesy of Fieldpiece Instruments.

capacitance, continuity, and temperature, in addition to
measuring voltage, resistance, and current. Some meters
are equipped with a feature referred to as an NCV tester,
which stands for non-contact voltage. This feature allows the
instrument user to detect voltage without actually having the
instrument come in physical contact with the circuit being
tested. To measure voltage and resistance, meters rely on test
leads, Figure 5.47. These test leads can be configured with
handheld probes or with alligator clips for ease of use.


---

110

Section 2 Safety, Tools and Equipment, and Shop Practices

Figure 5.50 Meter being used to measure amperage. Courtesy of Fluke
Corporation.

sensed in the vapor sample. Always make certain that the leak
detector being used is intended to be used with the refrigerant
that is to be detected. Varieties of electronic leak detectors
include the heated diode refrigerant leak detector, Figure!5.52,
the infrared refrigerant leak detector, Figure!5.53, and the carbon dioxide leak detector, Figure 5.54.
Figure 5.52 Heated diode refrigerant leak detector. Courtesy of Fieldpiece Instruments.

variation on the clamp-on ammeter is an instrument that can
measure circuit current by simply positioning the meters nonoperational jaws around the conductor, Figure 5.50.

#### 5.5 Refrigerant Leak
#### DETECTORS
#### Electronic Leak Detectors
Electronic leak detectors, Figure 5.51, contain an element sensitive to a particular refrigerant class. The instrument typically
has an internal pump that pulls vapor into the leak detector
and through a sensor. The leak detector evaluates the vapor
being pulled in to determine if refrigerant is present. An audio
beeping signal that increases in frequency when refrigerant is

Figure 5.53 Infrared refrigerant leak detector. Courtesy of Fieldpiece
Instruments.

Figure 5.51 Electronic leak detector. Source: Ritchie Engineering Company, Inc. Yellow Jacket Products Division.


---

## PDF Page 111

### Unit 5 Tools, Instrumentation, and!Equipment
Figure 5.54 Carbon dioxide leak detector. Courtesy of Fieldpiece
Instruments.

111

#### Ultrasonic Leak Detector
Ultrasonic refrigerant leak detectors use the sound from the
escaping refrigerant to detect a leak, Figure 5.56. Ultrasonic
leak detectors can be used on systems that are pressurized with
dry nitrogen, as the detector is not detecting the presence of particular chemicals, just the sound of moving gases within a pipe.

#### Halide Leak Detectors
A halide leak detector, Figure 5.57, uses an acetylene- or propane-fueled flame to heat a copper disk, which is then used to
Figure 5.56 (A) Ultrasonic leak detector. Courtesy of Robinair Division, SPX Corporation. (B) Ultrasonic leak detector. Courtesy of Amprobe
Instruments.

#### Ultraviolet Leak Detectors
The ultraviolet leak detection system, Figure 5.55, introduces
an additive into the refrigerant system. The additive shows
up as a bright yellow-green glow on the refrigerant piping at
the leak site when viewed under ultraviolet light. The additive
can remain in the system to test for a new suspected leak at
a later date. The ultraviolet lights that are used to inspect the
refrigerant piping are compact, handheld lights that resemble
small flashlights.
(A)
Figure 5.55 Ultraviolet leak detecting system. Courtesy of Spectronics.

(B)

## PDF Page 112

112

Section 2 Safety, Tools and Equipment, and Shop Practices

Figure 5.57 Halide leak detector. Courtesy of Uniweld Products.

Figure 5.59 Plastic tubing shear. Photo by Bill Johnson

detect chlorinated refrigerant gas leaks. When the detector is
ignited, the flame heats a copper disk and a green flame is produced. Air for combustion is drawn through the attached hose,
the end of which is passed over or near fittings or other areas
where a leak may be suspected. If there is a leak, the refrigerant
will be drawn into the hose and contact the copper disk. This
breaks down the halogen refrigerants into other compounds
and changes the color of the flame from green to blue.

cutter that allows the user to effectively cut tubing in tight
spaces. Tubing cutters are commonly used to cut copper and
aluminum tubing but can be used to cut rigid copper pipe as
well. Some models have a flare cut-off groove that reduces
tube loss when removing a cracked or damaged flare. Many
models also include a retractable reamer to remove inside
burrs, and a filing surface to remove outside burrs.
Another cutter that is used to cut plastic tubing and nonwire reinforced plastic or synthetic hose is the plastic tubing shear, Figure 5.59. This cutter has a ratcheting handle to
make the cutting process easier.

#### 5.6 Tubing Tools
#### Tubing Cutters

#### REAMERS

Tube cutters, Figure 5.58, are available in different sizes and
styles. The tubing cutter at the left of Figure 5.58 is a compact

Reamers, Figure 5.60, are used to remove burrs from sections of cut copper tubing. The inner-outer reamer shown in
Figure 5.60, with its three cutters, has the ability to ream
both the inside and outside of a tubing section. Other reamers, Figure!5.61, are compact, handy tools. Figure 5.62 shows
a technician using a reamer. Some tubing cutters are equipped
with reamers.

113

#### Inspection Mirrors

#### Flaring Tools

Inspection mirrors, Figure 5.63, are usually available in rectangular or round shapes, with stainless steel or glass reflective
surfaces that are usually mounted on ball joints that swivel
360 degrees. They come with fixed or telescoping handles,
some more than 30 in. long. The mirrors are used to inspect
areas or parts in components that are behind or underneath
other parts. Inspection mirrors are commonly used to inspect
newly soldered or brazed joints to locate improperly soldered
joints.

The flaring tool, Figure 5.65, has a flaring bar or block to
hold the tubing, a slip-on yoke, and a feed screw with flaring
cone and handle. Several sizes of tubing can be flared with
this tool. A completed flare resembles a bell-shaped bevel on
the end of the tubing. Tubing is flared so that it can be used
in conjunction with flare nuts. Refer to Unit 7 for detailed
information on the use of the flaring block.

#### Tubing Brushes

Swaging tools, Figure 5.66, are used to expand the end of
a section of soft-drawn tubing so that it can accept another
section of same size tubing, in preparation for soldering or
brazing. Refer to Unit 7 for detailed information on the use
of swaging tools.

Tubing brushes, Figure 5.64, are used to clean the outside
of tubing and the inside of fittings prior to soldering. The
brushes are sized to fit the tubing to be cleaned.

#### Swaging Tools

114

Section 2 Safety, Tools and Equipment, and Shop Practices

Figure 5.69 Ball-type, pinch-off tool. Photo by Bill Johnson

#### Tubing Benders
Tubing benders are used to bend, without kinking, sections
of soft-drawn tubing. Bending soft-drawn tubing is desirable
especially when installing refrigerant lines, as doing so greatly
reduces the number of soldered and/or brazed joints, thus
reducing the potential for system leaks. Two commonly used
types of tubing benders are the spring bender, Figure! 5.67,
and the lever bender, Figure 5.68. Refer to Unit 7 for detailed
information on the use of tubing benders.

#### Pinch-Off Tools
A tubing pinch-off tool is used to pinch shut an access or
process tube, such as the service stub on a hermetic compressor. The stub is pinched shut in preparation for being

soldered. The ball-type, pinch-off tool, Figure 5.69, creates
a rounded pinch-off, which is very sturdy. Figure 5.70 shows
two other types of pinch-off tools that create straight-line
pinch-offs.


---

### Unit 5 Tools, Instrumentation, and!Equipment

115

Figure 5.70 Straight-line pinch-off tools. Photo by Bill Johnson

Figure 5.73 Reamer used to remove burrs from the interior of pipe
sections. Photo by Eugene Silberstein

#### 5.7 Piping Tools

Figure 5.74 Pipe vise. Courtesy of Ridge Tool Company.

#### Pipe Wrenches
Pipe wrenches, Figure 5.71, are used to turn sections of rigid
pipe and related piping system components. These wrenches
come in a number of different sizes, based on the size of pipe
being turned. The long handles on these wrenches provide
the additional torque needed to effectively tighten or loosen
piping connections.

#### Pipe Cutters
A larger version of the tubing cutter, the pipe cutter,
Figure!5.72, is used to cut sections of rigid piping made from
materials such as steel.

#### Pipe Reamers
Pipe reamers are used to remove burrs from sections of rigid
piping. Figure 5.73 shows a technician using a reamer on a
newly cut section of black pipe.

#### Pipe Vises
Pipe vises, Figure 5.74, are used to secure sections of pipe so that
operations such as cutting, reaming, threading, and joining pipe
sections to other pipe sections or components can be performed.

Figure 5.71 Pipe wrench. Courtesy of Ridge Tool Company.

#### Pipe Threaders

Figure 5.72 Pipe cutter. Courtesy of Ridge Tool Company.

Pipe threaders are used to create external threads on a section
of rigid pipe. Figure 5.75 shows a manual pipe threader, which
is a ratcheting tool used to create the external threads. An automatic pipe threader, Figure 5.76, performs the threading process
Figure 5.75 Pipe threader. Courtesy of Ridge Tool Company.

116

Section 2 Safety, Tools and Equipment, and Shop Practices

Figure 5.76 Automatic pipe threader. Courtesy of Ridge Tool Company.

automatically and is often equipped with other commonly used
piping tools such as a pipe cutter and reamer.

Figure 5.78 Scratch awl. Photo by Eugene Silberstein

#### 5.8 Sheet Metal Tools
#### Tin Snips
Tin snips, Figure 5.77, are intended to cut sheet metal. Snips
are configured for left-turning cuts, right-turning cuts, and
straight cuts. Red tin snips are intended for straight and leftturning cuts, while green tin snips are intended for straight and
right-turning cuts. Yellow snips are typically used for straight
cutting, but are referred to as general purpose tin snips.

#### Scratch Awl
Although used for other tasks as well, the scratch awl,
Figure! 5.78, is commonly used when working with sheet
metal. This tool is used to scratch lines in the sheet metal to
mark the point where cuts, bends, or notches are to be made.

Figure 5.77 Tin snips. Red-handled snips for left-turning cuts (left),
yellow-handled snips for straight cuts (center), green-handled snips for
right-turning cuts (right). Photo by Eugene Silberstein

#### Crimpers And Notchers
Crimpers and notchers, Figure 5.79, are common tools used
by sheet metal workers. Crimpers are primarily used to create a ripple in the end of round stove pipe duct sections. This
ripple reduces the diameter of the end of the stove pipe so
that two sections of same-size stove pipe can be connected
together, Figure 5.80. Notchers are used to create uniform
notches, either square or triangular in shape, in pieces of
sheet metal. These notches are created for a number of reasons including creating clinch fittings and angled tabs for
slip- and drive-connected duct sections, Figure 5.81.

#### Hand Seamers And Folding Tools
Hand seamers and folding tools, Figure 5.82, are utility folding tools used by those working with sheet metal and duct
systems. These tools allow the user to create sharp bends in
sheet metal, such as when flanges need to be bent in plenums
to mount them onto air handlers.


---

### Unit 5 Tools, Instrumentation, and!Equipment

117

Figure 5.80 Sheet metal crimpers used to reduce the diameter of a section of round duct.

comes to the source of heat. The most compact and portable option is a propane gas torch with a disposable propane gas tank, Figure 5.83. This is an easy torch to use, as
the flame adjusts easily and can be used for many soldering operations. The propane fuel comes in small 14-ounce
tanks. Another option for these torches is what is known
as MAPP™ gas, which is a mixture of hydrocarbon fuels.
The propane torch flame is at a temperature of approximately 3600°F, while the MAPP™ gas flame is approximately 3730°F. The air-acetylene torch kit, Figure 5.84,
provides sufficient heat for soldering and brazing, as a

#### 5.9 Hvacr Installation
#### And Service Tools And
#### EQUIPMENT
#### TORCHES
Soldering and brazing are two tasks that are commonly
performed by the HVACR service technician. There are
many options that are available to the technician when it


---

## PDF Page 118

118

Section 2 Safety, Tools and Equipment, and Shop Practices

flame temperature of about 4200°F is produced. It consists
of a torch, which can be fitted with several sizes of tips,
and a regulator, hoses, and an acetylene tank. Oxyacetylene
torch kits, Figure 5.85, are used in certain soldering, brazing, and welding applications. The units consist of a torch,
regulators, Figure 5.86, hoses, oxygen tanks, and acetylene
gas tanks. When the oxygen and acetylene gases are mixed
in the proper proportion, a very hot flame, approximately
5600°F, is produced.
When brazing refrigerant lines, it is best field practice to
purge the refrigerant lines with nitrogen to prevent oxidation
in the lines. The desired nitrogen pressure is between 1 and
2 psig, which is a pressure that is too low to measure on a
gauge or regulator. For this reason, a nitrogen flow indicator, Figure 5.87, is useful to provide visual confirmation that
the correct amount of nitrogen is flowing through the piping
arrangement.

Figure 5.86 Oxygen and acetylene regulators. Photo by Eugene
Silberstein

Figure 5.87 Nitrogen flow indicator. Courtesy of Uniweld Products, Inc.

Figure 5.85 Oxyacetylene torch kit. Courtesy of Uniweld Products, Inc.

#### Gauges And Manifolds
One of the most important of all pieces of refrigeration and
air-conditioning service equipment is the gauge manifold.
Gauge manifolds can be analog or digital instruments. A


---

## PDF Page 119

### Unit 5 Tools, Instrumentation, and!Equipment
Figure 5.88 Four-valve gauge manifold. Source: Ritchie Engineering
Company, Inc. Yellow Jacket Products Division.

119

Figure 5.89 Digital gauge manifold. Courtesy of Fieldpiece Instruments.

Figure 5.90 Electronic, Gaugeless manifold. Courtesy of Northpark
Innovations.

typical gauge manifold, Figure 5.88, includes the compound
gauge (low-pressure and vacuum), the high-pressure gauge,
and the manifold, valves, and hoses. The gauge manifold may
be configured with two valves and three hoses or with four
valves and four hoses. The two-valve manifold has a highside hose port, a low-side hose port, and one center port.
The four-valve manifold has a high-side hose port, a low-side
hose port, and two center ports. Figure 5.88 shows a fourvalve manifold, which has separate valves for the vacuum,
low-pressure, high-pressure, and refrigerant cylinder connections. The vacuum hose on four-valve manifolds is 3/8 in. in
diameter, while the other hoses are 1/4 in. in diameter. This
larger hose offers less restriction to flow for faster evacuation. Digital manifolds, such as the one shown in Figure 5.89,
in addition to being able to measure pressures, has the ability
to measure temperatures and calculate evaporator superheat
and condenser subcooling.
Some electronic manifolds are also available that do
not have physical gauges on them at all. The manifold in
Figure!5.90 utilizes a smart device application and can measure system pressures and temperatures, calculate superheat
and subcooling, and measure vacuum level in microns. It
may also be used as a digital thermometer. Whenever hoses
are connected to a system, they must be equipped with lowloss fittings, Figure 5.91. These fittings reduce the amount of
refrigerant that is lost during the installation or removing of
gauges from a system.

Some air-conditioning and refrigeration service gauges
do not have a manifold. Figure 5.92 shows a set of digital,
wireless, Bluetooth capable, stub gauges that can be connected directly to the service ports to obtain system pressure
readings.
Another gauge that is commonly used by the HVACR service technician is the vacuum, or micron gauge. The micron
gauge is used to measure the vacuum, in microns, created
during the evacuation process. This micron gauge shown in
Figure 5.93 is a digital, wireless, stand-alone stub gauge that


---

120

pressure gauges. Courtesy of Appion Inc.

has Bluetooth capabilities for remote monitoring. The micron
gauge gets connected to the system in a similar manner as the
stub gauges discussed earlier, Figure 5.94.
SAFETY PRECAUTION: Always make certain that the
gauges, hoses, and other instrumentation being used is rated for
the system’s expected operating pressures. Serious personal injury
could result if system pressures exceed the pressure ratings on connected devices. -

#### Performance Instrumentation
Other specialty instruments are available that assist the
HVACR service technician in evaluating and troubleshooting equipment. Some of these devices aid in the calculation of evaporator superheat and condenser subcooling,

Figure! 5.95. Other instruments are application-based and
allow the technician to obtain pressure, temperature, and air
property measurements to effectively evaluate system performance, Figure 5.96.

#### Charging Scales
Digital, programmable refrigerant charging scales allow a
technician to accurately introduce refrigerant to HVACR
systems by weight. This can usually be done manually or

automatically, and the amount of refrigerant to be charged
into the system can be programmed. Figure 5.97 is an example of a programmable charging scale that is equipped with
an alarm and Figure 5.98 shows a wireless refrigerant scale.
When charging systems with blended refrigerants, the refrigerant must leave the tank as a liquid. To prevent liquid refrigerant from potentially damaging the system compressor, a
liquid restrictor, Figure 5.99, can be used.

#### Vacuum Pumps
Vacuum pumps, Figure 5.100, are designed to evacuate airconditioning and refrigeration systems by removing air and


---

## PDF Page 122

122

Section 2 Safety, Tools and Equipment, and Shop Practices

Figure 5.99 Liquid restrictor. Photo by Bill Johnson

Figure 5.101 Recovery unit. Courtesy of Appion.

Figure 5.100 8 cfm vacuum pump. Courtesy of Appion.

#### Recovery Units
The recovery unit, Figure 5.101, is used to remove refrigerant
from HVACR systems. It is illegal to intentionally vent refrigerant into the atmosphere. The refrigerant from a refrigerator
or air-conditioning system is pumped into a cylinder or container until it can be either charged back into the system or is
surrendered to a refrigerant reclaiming facility.

#### Fin Straighteners
Fin straighteners, Figure 5.102, are used to straighten the fins
on condenser and evaporator coils. The spacing between coil
fins changes, so the right straightener needs to be used.

#### THERMOMETERS

other noncondensable gases, as well as moisture, from the
system. Evacuating the system is necessary because the air
and noncondensable gases take up space, contain moisture,
and cause excessive pressures. Vacuum pumps may be either
one- or two-stage and are rated in cubic feet per minute or
cfm. The two-stage pump is the one most often recommended
for use. It will create a lower vacuum to improve the system
vacuum in situations where moisture is an issue.

Thermometers are used extensively by the HVACR service
technician. These devices can be simple, single probe devices,
Figure 5.103, or can be multiprobe instruments with the ability
to internally calculate temperature differentials, Figure!5.104.
Infrared thermometers, Figure 5.105, are also popular. Be sure
to use thermometers according to manufacturer’s instructions
to ensure the required accuracy is achieved.

#### Wheel Puller
Wheel pullers, Figure 5.106, are used for removing propeller
type fan blades, squirrel cage type blower wheels, and pulleys
and sheaves.

#### Core Removal Tool
The core removal tool, Figure 5.109, allows the service technician to remove and replace Schrader cores without removing the refrigerant charge. Schrader core removal allows for
less restriction when evacuating a system allowing for a faster
and deeper vacuum.

#### Tank Heater

#### REFRACTOMETER

The tank heater, Figure 5.107, is used to add heat and pressure
to refrigerant tanks for faster charging especially on cold days.

The refractometer, Figure 5.110, is used for retrofitting a
mineral oil system with POE lubricant. This tool can accurately predict remaining mineral oil in the system down to as
low as 1%.

#### Cable Tie Tool
The cable tie tool, Figure 5.108, is used to install nylon strap
clamps around a flexible duct. This tool automatically cuts
the clamping strap off flush when a preset tension is reached.
Figure 5.108 Tool used to install nylon strap clamps around a flexible
duct. Photo by Bill Johnson

#### Oil Pumps
Oil pumps, Figure 5.111, are used to manually transfer oil
into and out of HVACR system compressors.
Figure 5.110 Handheld refractometer. Source: Ritchie Engineering
Company, Inc. Yellow Jacket Products Division.

#### MANOMETERS
Manometers are used to measure very low pressures and
express these pressure readings in inches of water column,
where 27.7 IWC is equal to 1 psi of pressure. Manometers are available as single-port devices, Figure 5.114, or

the air analyzer, Figure 5.112, and the microanemometer,

Figure 5.115 Dual-port manometer. Courtesy of Fieldpiece Instruments.

Figure 5.117 Digital psychrometer. Photo by Eugene Silberstein.

Figure 5.118 Flow hood. Source: Alnor Instrument Company.

dual-port devices, Figure 5.115. Manometers are commonly
used to measure the pressures (total, static, and velocity) in
duct systems as well as fuel pressures in gas-fired heating
appliances.

#### PSYCHROMETERS
Psychrometers are used to measure the wet-bulb and drybulb temperatures to determine the relative humidity of
an airstream. A sling psychrometer, Figure 5.116, uses two
thermometers, a dry-bulb and a wet-bulb thermometer. The
thermometers are whirled together in the air to obtain the
wet-bulb and dry-bulb temperatures. From these temperatures, the relative humidity can be determined.
Digital psychrometers, Figure 5.117, are also available.
These devices are similar to the sling psychrometer, but these
devices do not have to be spun in an air stream and often
provide more information about the air sample, including, in
some instances, enthalpy.

Figure 5.116 Sling psychrometer. Photo by Bill Johnson.

#### Flow Hoods
Flow hoods, Figure 5.118, are used to measure airflow, in
cfm, through supply and return registers and grilles.

#### 5.11 Combustion Testing
#### EQUIPMENT
#### Carbon Dioxide And Oxygen Test
#### INSTRUMENTS
Carbon dioxide (CO2) and oxygen (O2) test instruments
are used to analyze flue gases to determine the combustion efficiency of gas- or oil-fired furnaces, Figure 5.119.
The test kit shown in Figure 5.119 is referred to as a “wet”
kit. Digital combustion analyzers, such as the one shown in
Figure!5.120, have the ability to measure and display concentrations in flue gases of oxygen (O2), carbon monoxide


---

## PDF Page 127

### Unit 5 Tools, Instrumentation, and!Equipment
Figure 5.119 Carbon dioxide and oxygen indicators. Source: Bacharach, Inc. Pittsburgh, PA, USA

127

#### Carbon Monoxide (Co) Detectors
A CO detector is used to determine the percentage of CO
present, Figure 5.122.

Figure 5.121 Combustion analyzer printer. Source: Bacharach, Inc.
Pittsburgh, PA, USA

Figure 5.120 Digital combustion analyzer. Source: Bacharach, Inc. Pittsburgh, PA, USA

Figure 5.122 Carbon monoxide (CO) detector. A technician checking a
furnace for carbon monoxide spillage with a digital carbon monoxide test
instrument. Courtesy of Fluke Corporation.

(CO), oxides of nitrogen (NOx), and sulfur dioxide (SO2).
They can also measure and display stack gas temperature,
while computing and displaying combustion efficiency,
stack loss, and excess air. These digital devices are very
popular, but they do need to be factory calibrated each
year. Some analyzers have the ability to print the results
of the combustion test for office records as well as for the
customer, Figure 5.121.

## PDF Page 128

128

Section 2 Safety, Tools and Equipment, and Shop Practices

Figure 5.123 Combustible gas leak detector. Courtesy of UEi.

Figure 5.124 Draft gauge. Source: Bacharach, Inc. Pittsburgh, PA, USA.

Figure 5.125 Smoke tester. Photo by Bill Johnson

#### Combustible Gas Leak Detector
The combustible gas leak detector, Figure 5.123, has an
audible alarm and provides visual leak detection by means of
light-emitting diode (LED) indicators. A red LED light on the
tip illuminates the search area.

#### Draft Gauges
A draft gauge, Figure 5.124, is used to check the pressure
of the flue gas in gas and oil furnaces to ensure that the flue
gases are moving up the flue at a satisfactory speed. The flue
gas is normally at a slightly negative pressure.

#### Smoke Testers
The smoke tester, Figure 5.125, is used to determine the
amount of soot that is contained in the flue gases of an oilfired heating appliance. Using the tool, a predetermined
amount of flue gases is drawn through a filter paper, which
is then compared to a color scale to determine the degree of
sooting that is taking place. It is strongly recommended that
a smoke test be performed before a combustion test is conducted to protect the instrumentation from damage caused
by excessive smoke. Refer to Unit 32 for more information
on the procedure to properly perform a smoke test.

#### 5.12 Energy Auditing
#### INSTRUMENTATION
Although many tools and instruments have been discussed
throughout this unit, the following three pieces of test
equipment are very popular when evaluating the integrity of the air distribution (duct) system and the building
envelope.

#### Thermal Imaging Cameras
Thermal imaging cameras, Figure 5.126, are used to capture heat signatures as visible light. This allows the energy
auditor to identify areas where heat is either escaping from
a heated structure, or leaking into a cooled structure. These


---

## PDF Page 129

### Unit 5 Tools, Instrumentation, and!Equipment
Figure 5.126 Infrared thermal imaging camera. Courtesy of Fluke
Corporation.

129

instruments are extremely useful because they have the ability to detect changes in temperature through walls and other
solid objects. Refer to Unit 39 for more information on how
thermal imaging cameras are used.

#### Duct Pressurization Test
#### EQUIPMENT
Duct pressurization equipment, Figure 5.127, is used to
pressurize, or de-pressurize, air distribution systems to
determine how tight the duct system is. Refer to Unit 39
for more information on how duct pressurization equipment is used.

#### Blower Door Test Equipment
Blower door test equipment, Figure 5.128, is used by an
energy auditor to determine how tight or how leaky a structure’s envelope is. Refer to Unit 39 for more information on
how blower door test equipment is used.

Figure 5.127 A technician performing a duct pressurization test on a
residential furnace. Courtesy of The Energy Conservatory.

Figure 5.128 A technician performing a blower door test on a residential dwelling. Courtesy of The Energy Conservatory.

the right fastener or system to securely install and mount all equipment,

#### 6.1 Nails
Probably the most common fastener used in wood is the nail.
Nails are available in many styles and sizes. Common nails
are large, flat-headed wire nails with a specific diameter for
each length, Figure!6.1(A). A finishing nail, Figure!6.1(B), has
a very small head so that it can be driven below the surface of
the wood; it is used where a good finish is desired.
The term penny, which originated in England, is used
to size nails. Ten-penny nails, for example, cost 10 pence

(A)

Section 2 Safety, Tools and Equipment, and Shop Practices

#### 6.2 Staples And Rivets

#### RIVETS

#### STAPLES

Pin rivets or blind rivets are used to join two pieces of sheet
metal. They are actually hollow rivets assembled on a pin,
often called a mandrel. Figure! 6.6 illustrates a pin rivet
assembly, how it is used to join sheet metal, and a riveting
tool or gun. The rivets are inserted and set from only one side
of the metal. Thus, they are particularly useful when there is
no access to the back side of the metal being fastened.
To use a pin rivet, you first drill a hole in the metal the
size of the rivet and insert the pin rivet in the hole with the
pointed end facing out. Then you place the nozzle of the riveting tool over the pin and squeeze the handles. Jaws grab the
pin and pull the head of the pin into the rivet, expanding it
and forming a head on the other side. Continue squeezing the
handles until the head on the reverse side forms a tight joint.
When this happens, the pin breaks off and is ejected from the
riveting tool. Figure! 6.7 shows what a finished rivet looks
like. The rivet can only be removed by drilling it out with a
drill bit that is the same size as the rivet. When the rivet is
drilled out, the back side of the rivet will fall on the inside of

A staple, Figure!6.3, is a fastener made somewhat like a nail
but shaped like a U with a point on each end. Staples are
available in different sizes for different uses, one of which is
to fasten wire in place. The staples are simply hammered in
over the wire.
SAFETY PRECAUTION: Never hammer staples too tightly because they can damage the wire.- Other types
of staples may be fastened in place with a stapling tacker,
Figure! 6.4. By depressing the handle of a tacker, a staple
can be driven through paper, fabric, or insulation into the
wood. Outward clinch tackers will anchor staples inside soft
materials. The staple legs spread outward to form a strong,
tight clinch, Figure! 6.5. They can be used to fasten insulation around heating or cooling pipes and ducts and to install
ductboard. Other models or tackers are used to staple lowvoltage wiring to wood.
Figure 6.3 Staples used to fasten wire to wood.

Figure 6.6 (A) A pin rivet assembly. (B) Using pin rivets to fasten sheet
metal. (C) A riveting gun. (A) and (B) Courtesy of Duro Dyne Corp. (C) Photo
by Eugene Silberstein

(A)

#### 6.3 Screws
#### Wood Screws
Wood screws are used to fasten many types of materials
to wood. They generally have a flat, round, or oval head,
Figure!6.8. The portion of flat- and oval-head screws beneath
the head is at an angle. Holes for these screws should
be countersunk so that the angular portion is recessed.
Wood screws can also have a straight, slotted head or a
Phillips recessed head.
Wood screw sizes are specified by length (in inches) and
shank diameter (a number from 0 to 24). The larger the number, the larger the diameter or gauge of the shank. The number that represents the shank diameter can be converted to
inches by using the following formula:

Tapping or sheet metal screws are used extensively by service technicians. These screws may have a straight slot, a
Phillips head, or a straight slot and hex head, Figure! 6.10.
To use a tapping screw, you drill a hole into the sheet metal
the approximate size of the root diameter of the thread. Then
you turn the screw into the hole with a conventional screwdriver or electric drill with a screwdriver bit.
Figure! 6.11 shows a tapping screw, often called a selfdrilling screw, that can be turned into sheet metal with an
electric drill using a chuck similar to the one shown. This
screw has a special point used to start the hole. It can be
used with light to medium gauges of sheet metal. Note that
self-tapping and self-drilling screws are not the same. Selftapping screws require a hole to be drilled in the sheet metal
prior to installing the screw; the self-drilling screw makes its
own hole. Notice the difference in the tips of the screws in
Figure!6.10 and Figure 6.11.
Figure 6.9 Lag screw or bolt. Photo by Eugene Silberstein

Shank Diameter in Inches 5 (Shank Number 3 0.013) 1 0.06
For example, a number 8 screw has a larger shank diameter
(0.1643) than a number 6 screw (0.1383). An 8-10 wood screw
and a 6-10 wood screw are both 1!in. long, but the number 8

#### Set Screws
Set screws have points, as illustrated in Figure!6.13, and have
square heads, hex heads, or no heads. The headless type may be
slotted for a screwdriver, or it may have a hexagonal or fluted
socket. These screws are used to keep a pulley from turning on
a shaft and for other, similar applications. The set screw that
is used to hold a pulley tight on a shaft is made of hardened
steel; the pulley shaft is made of soft steel. When the set screw
is tightened onto the shaft, it indents the shaft with its tip.

#### Fastener Heads
Figure! 6.14 is an illustration of several fastener heads and
their driver tips.
Figure 6.13 Styles of set screws.

Tapping screws are identified or labeled in a specific way.
The following is a typical identifying label:
6-20 3 1/2 Type AB, Slotted hex
6 5 Screw Size Number
20 5 Number of threads per inch
1/2 5 Length of the screw
Type AB 5 Type of point
Slotted hex 5 Type of head

#### Machine Screws
There are many types of machine screws. They normally are
identified by their head styles. Some of these are indicated in
Figure!6.12. A thread dimension or label indicates the following information:
5/16–18 UNC -2
5/16 5 Outside thread diameter
18 5 Number of threads per inch
UNC 5 Unified thread series (Unified National Coarse)
2 5 Class of "t (the amount of play between the
internal and external threads)

#### Wall Anchors

wallboard, gypsum board, and similar materials. Once the
anchor has been set, the screw may be removed as often as
necessary without affecting the anchor.

screwing the toggle on the bolt, Figure!6.17. Push the toggle

Section 2 Safety, Tools and Equipment, and Shop Practices

#### Plastic Toggle Drywall Anchors
One variation on the toggle bolt is the plastic toggle, Figure!6.20. Once a hole of the proper size has been made in the
gypsum board, the plastic toggle is folded in half and inserted
into the hole, Figure!6.21. A special pin that comes with the
anchor is inserted in the anchor to ensure that it opens inside
the wall, Figure!6.22. Once the anchor is in place, the screw
is fed through the item being secured and tightened into the
anchor, Figure!6.23. Unlike the traditional toggle bolt, constant tension on the anchor is not required for tightening.

Figure 6.22 (A) Using a pin to open the toggle. (B) Back view of opened
plastic toggle. Photos by Eugene Silberstein

#### Self-Drilling Drywall Anchors
Another option for drywall applications is the self-drilling drywall anchor, Figure!6.24. This type of anchor does not require
a predrilled hole. You simply press the tip of the anchor into
the gypsum board and turn it clockwise until it is flush with
the surface, Figure! 6.25. Once the anchor is in place, the
screw is fed through the item being secured and tightened
into the anchor.

(A)

#### Anchor Shields
Anchor shields with bolts or screws are used to fasten objects to masonry or, in some instances, hollow
walls. Figure! 6.27 illustrates a multipurpose steel anchor
bolt used in masonry material. You drill a hole in the
masonry the size of the sleeve, tap the sleeve and bolt
into the hole with a hammer, and turn the bolt head. This
expands the sleeve and secures the bolt in the masonry,
Figure!6.28. A variety of head styles are available.

#### Lag Shield Anchors
Figure 6.25 Installing the self-drilling drywall anchor. Photo by Eugene
Silberstein

A two-part shield, generally used with lag screws, has
tapered internal threads for part of its length, Figure!6.29.
To install them, drill a hole of the diameter recommended by
the manufacturer for the size of shield. The hole should be
drilled as deep as the length of the shield plus approximately
Figure 6.27 An anchor shield with a screw. Courtesy of Rawlplug Company, Inc.

Figure 6.28 Image showing the anchor shield before and after expansion. Photo by Eugene Silberstein

#### 6.5 Concrete Fasteners
#### Screw Fasteners For Concrete
A concrete screw fastener may be used in poured concrete,
concrete block, or brick, Figure! 6.26. A hole must first be
drilled in the base material. It is important to use the drill
diameter recommended by the manufacturer for each screw
size. Drill the hole slightly deeper than the length of the
screw that will extend into the base material. Blow out the
dust and other material from the hole to the extent possible.
SAFETY PRECAUTION: Wear safety goggles during this drilling and cleaning process.- The screw may then be installed
Figure 6.29 A lag shield anchor.
Figure 6.26 A concrete screw fastener.

Section 2 Safety, Tools and Equipment, and Shop Practices

1/2!in. Clean dust or loose concrete material out of the hole.
SAFETY PRECAUTION: Wear goggles when drilling and
cleaning.- Tap the shield in with a hammer until it is flush
with the surface. Position the material to be fastened with
the hole over the shield and turn in the lag screw.

#### Powder-Actuated Fastener
#### SYSTEMS
These systems provide another method of fastening materials
to concrete, Figure!6.30.
SAFETY PRECAUTION: This type
of system is very dangerous. Operators must have specific training
and in many instances must be licensed. We only mention it here
to make you aware that there is such a system.- A powder load,
Figure!6.31, is used in a powder-actuated tool (PAT). When
actuated, it forces a pin, threaded stud, or other fastener into
the concrete. The powder loads are color-coded based on the
amount of power needed for a particular job.
#### SAFETY
PRECAUTION: Do not try this without the training and the license
to operate this equipment.-
Many other types of fasteners are used to fasten material
to concrete. Those described here are but a few examples.

#### 6.6 Threaded Rod
#### Threaded Rod And Angle Steel
Threaded rod and angle steel can be used to make custom
hangers for pipes or components such as an air handler, Figure!6.32. Threaded rod can be attached to steel I-beams with
a beam clamp, Figure! 6.33.
SAFETY PRECAUTION: Be
sure that all materials are strong enough to adequately support
the equipment.-

#### Threaded Rod And Steel Channel
Threaded rod can also be used in combination with steel channel to fabricate custom hanging arrangements, Figure!6.34.

#### 6.7 Piping And Ductwork
#### FASTENERS
#### Pipe Hook
A wire pipe hook is a hardened steel wire bent into a U with
a point at an angle on both ends. The pipe or tubing rests in
the bottom of the U, and the pointed ends are driven into
wooden joists or other wood supports, Figure!6.38.

#### Pipe Strap
(B)

A pipe strap is used for fastening pipe and tubing to joists,
ceilings, or walls, Figure!6.39(A). The arc on the strap should
be approximately the same as the outside diameter of the

collar. Courtesy of Panduit Corporation

(A)

#### Nylon Strap
(B)

Figure 6.39 (A) A pipe strap. (B) Perforated strapping material.

To fasten round flexible duct to a sheet metal collar, apply
sealer to the inner liner of the duct and clamp it with a nylon
strap. A special tool is manufactured to install this strap,
which applies the correct tension and cuts the strap off. The
insulation and vapor barrier are positioned over the collar,
which can then be secured with another nylon strap. Duct
tape is applied over this strap and duct end to further seal the
system. Figure!6.40 illustrates this procedure.

#### 6.8 Pins, Clamps, And Rings
#### Cotter Pins And Hairpins

pipe or tubing. These straps are normally fastened with
round-head screws. Notice that the underside of the head of
a round-head screw is flat and provides good contact with
the strap.

Cotter pins, Figure!6.41, are commonly used to secure objects
on shafts. The cotter pin is inserted through the hole in the
shaft and the ends of the pin are spread to keep it in place,
Figure!6.42. Another type of clip that is similar to the cotter
pin is the hairpin, Figure 6.43. This type of pin is also inserted
through a hole in a shaft, but the configuration of the pin
holds it in place, Figure 6.44.
Figure 6.41 Cotters pin. Photo by Eugene Silberstein

#### Perforated Strap
A perforated strap may also be used to support pipe and tubing. Round-head stove bolts with nuts fasten the straps to
themselves, and round-head wood screws fasten the straps
to wood supports, Figure!6.39(B). These straps are so named
because of the many holes that are provided in the material.
These holes allow the strap to be used on pipes of many different sizes.

#### Retaining Rings
Retaining rings, also referred to as snap-rings, are fasteners
that hold a component either onto a shaft or inside some
type of housing. Rings that are used to hold a wheel on a

shaft, for example, are referred to as external retaining rings,
Figure!6.45, while those that are used to keep a component
inside a shaft or other housing are called internal retaining
rings, Figure 6.46. Retaining rings are intended to be positioned inside grooves in a shaft, Figure 6.47. When properly
installed, the portion of the ring that extends past the shaft
creates a shoulder that holds the wheel or component in place.
Special pliers are used to remove and install retaining rings,
Figure 6.48. The pair of pliers in Figure 6.48 has interchangeable heads that allow the user to get into tight areas. In addition, the pliers has two sets of pins on which a head can be
